How Stripe Built Their New Website

Y Combinator(2.2M) ·43:37 ·5.0K views · 2026-04-22
Aaron Epstein (Design Partner, Y Combinator) Katie Dill (Head of Design, Stripe)
Stripe’s homepage redesign highlights a shift from a payment-centric narrative to a comprehensive "financial manifesto," utilizing custom internal tools and AI to achieve a level of craft that signals technical reliability to global enterprises.
TL;DR Stripe’s homepage redesign highlights a shift from a payment-centric narrative to a comprehensive "financial manifesto," utilizing custom internal tools and AI to achieve a level of craft that signals technical reliability to global enterprises. Business Evolution:...

TL;DR

Stripe’s homepage redesign highlights a shift from a payment-centric narrative to a comprehensive "financial manifesto," utilizing custom internal tools and AI to achieve a level of craft that signals technical reliability to global enterprises.

Strategic Shift: From Feature-Adding to a Unified Narrative

  • Business Evolution: Stripe’s previous site (launched in 2020) survived six years, but the business outgrew the "add-on" structure. The redesign moved away from simply stacking sections to a cohesive story targeting both micro-entrepreneurs and multinational enterprises.
  • The "Website as Manifesto": Head of Design Katie Dill notes that a website serves as an indirect manifesto; the level of care in the UI signals the level of care Stripe takes in moving money and protecting sensitive data.
  • AI Market Dominance: A primary goal was highlighting Stripe’s role in the AI economy—over 78% of the Forbes AI 50 currently use Stripe to monetize via usage-based billing and global scaling.

New UI Paradigms: The "Bento" and Live Social Proof

  • Live GDP Counter: Stripe utilizes a live counter of the global GDP processed through its systems. This is a "power move" unique to Stripe that immediately signals scale, dependability, and trust to large-scale users.
  • The "Bento" Box: To solve the problem of product breadth, Stripe implemented a visual grid (the "bento") featuring:
      • Progressive Disclosure: Instead of navigating users away from the homepage, Stripe uses large modal overlays to provide product depth while keeping the user in a "lean-back" browsing mode.
      • Purposeful Motion: Animations (e.g., usage-based billing lines) are fine-tuned to feel "alive" without being distracting, serving as feedback that elements are interactive.
  • "Squeezy Boy" Section: A flexible, dynamically sized area designed for changing content like the "Black Friday Cyber Monday" city or the "Cheeky Pint" podcast, maintaining visual interest regardless of window scale.

The Design Process: Custom Tools and AI Integration

  • The Wave Tool: Stripe engineers built a custom internal tool to allow designers to manipulate grain, texture, blur, and rotation of their signature "gradient wave." This allowed for "infinite variations" before settling on a version that felt timeless.
  • AI in Creative Production:
      • Iterative Imagery: Stripe used AI to generate complex brand imagery (like the Stripe parallelogram integrated into customer brand photos).
      • The "Bubble" Critique: Dill shared internal critiques showing that even AI-generated "realism" (like ice cube bubbles) required human intervention to fix anatomical errors and lighting nuances.
  • Raising the Floor: Dill believes AI "raises the floor" for design, allowing teams to hit a "7 out of 10" quality level instantly. This frees designers to spend their time on new paradigms, such as "agent experiences" (AI interacting with AI).

Culture of Quality: "Walking the Store"

  • Fighting Mediocrity: Dill warns against the "gravitational pull to mediocrity," where teams accept "good enough" to save time. Stripe’s culture is built on "MVQP" (Minimum Viable Quality Product) rather than just MVP.
  • Essential Journeys: Every Friday, Stripe founders and leaders "walk the store"—a practice of live-testing user journeys in front of the company to find "dead ends" and ensure cross-product cohesion.
  • Prototyping vs. Presenting: The design team prioritizes high-fidelity prototypes over static presentations. This allows stakeholders to experience the product exactly as a user would, removing the need for explanatory "trade-off" narratives.
"The gravitational pull is to mediocrity. It is so easy to accept 'good enough,' but you have to think about the cost. If you make that decision every day, what is that composite going to be?"

— Katie Dill, Stripe Head of Design

"Don’t be wooed by just how easy that was to achieve with an AI tool... ask yourself: 'Is this really great? Does it feel well-crafted?' You should not accept slop."

— Katie Dill, Stripe Head of Design

"What is the point of a website anyway? I remember being in a meeting where Patrick [Collison] asked that. It’s your manifesto... demonstrating who you are, what you are doing, and why you do it."

— Katie Dill, Stripe Head of Design

Today, I am thrilled to welcome Stripe's Head of Design, Katie Dill, back to the show. Katie and her team recently launched a brand-new Stripe homepage. She's going to take us behind the scenes of the new website and talk about how AI is changing her team's design process. Welcome to another episode of Design Review. Katie, thank you so much for joining us again. Well, thank you for having me. It's nice to be back. I saw, along with everybody else, when you launched the new Stripe homepage. I thought it would be amazing to have you on and kind of take us through the old site and things that you wanted to change about it and update, because I know it's been a few years since you launched that one. And then take us through the new one and talk a little bit about the process that you used in order to get to where you guys landed with the new one. I do enjoy a good design crit with you, so let's do it. All right, let's jump into the original site. So how long ago did you launch this one? This one was from 2020. Okay, so we're talking six years ago at this point. Kudos to the team. I wasn't there at the time, but I do think that they built something that stood the test of time quite well. We weren't saying, "Oh my gosh, we have to change it, it's been too long, it's just, you know, it doesn't work anymore." It actually was standing up quite well. When we endeavored to change it, it was because the business had just evolved beyond the point of what story was being told on this site. I'll explain that a little bit. Of course, we wanted to kind of freshen it up as a part of our brand that also had been evolving and becoming more sophisticated and reaching a broader set of users. But still, it was a good site. So I can point out a couple of things that we aimed to change. One, this evolved over time. We certainly had changed the words on the site and exactly how it was portraying what we do. And then also some of the product shots that we were including. And then, of course, our users as we continue to evolve and help more in different categories or larger enterprises or growing startups. But then as you go down the page, we were articulating the number of things that we do. This is actually one of the things that had evolved the most over the last couple of years because our product suite was evolving. For example, we started with payments being a main part of our business, but over time, payments is just one part of it. We now today serve multinational enterprises in many categories including helping them with subscriptions, helping AI companies with usage-based billing, tax, revenue recognition, and platforms like Shopify helping them move money for the businesses on their platform. And so these stories were coming through here, but you were kind of only getting a glimpse of the product offering that Stripe provided. This was probably the biggest thing that we realized that we needed to change. We needed to adjust that. And the other piece is that the page grows quite long. There's a lot of trying to be told here. We were doing that thing that happens over time where you kind of add on and add on, and we're kind of losing the narrative with the site. So we thought to think about ways that we could update that narrative. Now, of course, you could adjust what was here, but we realized that it was also time to refresh the visuals. I don't think that the visuals came out of fashion, but it also just didn't feel like it took the sophistication of how the business itself evolved. We were seeing it honestly in the way that our teams were kind of rolling out this design language. There wasn't a lot of clarity on how do we use this 12-degree bar, how do we use colors and overlays, or how do we use our typography. The gradient wave that's at the top was certainly an element, but it didn't really scale well. So we had to create a new design vision for our language and then update the story. It's interesting because you mentioned that visually, I look at this and this looks like a site that could have been launched yesterday. I think that's a testament to how Stripe has always been so far ahead of the game from a design perspective that you could have a site that's six years old. Obviously, you made updates to it over the years; this wasn't literally the site that you launched six years ago, but clearly it stood the test of time, which is really impressive. And it makes sense that you've got all these different customers now. It seems like the approach that you took with the old one was just keep adding sections that would target different types of users and hopefully people would scroll until they hit the one that's right for them. And it sounds like maybe you've rethought that for the new site that you launched. Yeah, I mean, perfect example: AI is a very big part of our business. It's both how we build, what we build, and who we build for. Over 78% of the Forbes AI 50 use Stripe. They are using our products to help them scale really fast and help them monetize in new ways. Take usage-based billing so that you can make the right moves for your inference costs and how your users are actually using your product. Before, it was just a tile on the website. So the story was missing. What we're actually building wasn't coming clear. When we realized, "Okay, now it's time to update our site," I would say there's a couple of things on that front. One is first principles: what are we trying to do here? Like, what is the point of a website anyway? I remember being in a meeting where Patrick asked, "Well, what is the point of a website?" I think one part of it is it's your manifesto, whether you explicitly call it that or not. Because you're demonstrating who you are, what you are doing, and why you do it. In some indirect ways, it's just the nature of the way that you build your site, how you present yourself, what colors you choose, what typography you choose, the details that you choose to care about and the ones you don't. And then of course, how you articulate what you're doing and what you offer. So we set out to make sure that the story was extremely clear for who we're serving and how we're serving them and what we care about. And so that's where, honestly, painstaking hours over a year or more is what ended up going into this. Partly because in some ways we weren't rushing to get something out. When we're building something for our users, it's like, okay, it needs to be out and it needs to be out tomorrow because they're dependent on this. This was for ourselves in a lot of ways and so we certainly could take the time to sniff out the right design and get on that scent. You know what, there were many weeks and months of design exploration where we liked it, but we're like, "No, that's not it yet. We're not ready to wear that shirt for the next six years or whatever it might be." And so it's financial infrastructure to grow your revenue is the first one, which is the exact same first sentence. So that has remained consistent and it seems like what you wanted to add here was the extra things here that would catch other people where Stripe would actually be the right product for them too. Yeah. One of the things that's interesting is I like looking at sites and trying to figure out what is something that only this company could do that nobody else could do. And one of the things that stands out to me is the GDP counter that you have here. Not many companies could have a live counter of the percentage of the global GDP, not even just US GDP, that is running on Stripe. Talk about the decision to put that right up at the top as well. Well, I would imagine a lot of people watching and people building websites are thinking about, well, how can we demonstrate the impact that we're having, the reason why you should trust us, the social proof, what users trust us before. These are important messages. They help the potential customer understand whether or not this is a brand that you can trust and okay, good to know that you've done it for others. As I expressed earlier, we're trying to help everybody from a micro-entrepreneur who's going to grow a startup into hopefully great success as well as these large enterprises. It matters a lot to them whether or not Stripe is dependable, reliable, trustworthy and can operate at their scale. So we asked ourselves, "Well, how do we express that? How do we help you see that we can?" Of course, one of the ways is that we tell you about the users that use us and you can see that scrolling across the bottom. But another way is the actual enormous impact that we have had the privilege and the responsibility of serving, and that can actually be quantified in the GDP. Yeah, I love that. And the other keyword here too is billionth. You know, like that just signals if you do billions of transactions Stripe is right for you. Yeah, YC companies. You've got this. Yeah, very cool. Okay, take us through the other sections that you put underneath that. Yeah, all right. So this next section is what we call our bento. This is probably the big area that's seeking to solve some of the problems I talked about earlier about really expressing kind of like the scale of the product offering. Before you saw, "Okay, we do issuing, we do payments," but here we're trying to depict a little bit more of the solution set. Payments, yes, we do payments; we do online payments, we also do terminal. Billing is what we were talking about earlier about usage-based billing or it could be fixed fee subscriptions. Any number of these types of things are a lot more adjustable and customizable to the businesses we're serving. We talked a little bit about the role that AI plays in organizations and so we wanted to make that clear. Our issuing product, stablecoins, and then of course, the way that we provide for platform businesses and marketplaces. What we're trying to do here is not overwhelm you with too much going on. I know there is a lot on this page, but you can see that there's very little text. We're just trying to get the point across to give you enough of a sense of like, "Okay, this may be for me." What we're really trying to do is show rather than tell. Hence the imagery. And then the quick way that you can gain more insight is we do this kind of overlay, this larger modal, so you don't actually have to go off your course. We're not taking you away from the homepage yet, but we can give you a little bit more of a sense of what this is all about. There's many products layered into this, right? On that bento it's one, two, three, four, five, six. But I think we have many, many more dozen products on top of that that we can't express all on one page. That would just be too much and it would overwhelm you. So we want to give you essentially progressive disclosure as a way of getting towards the more details behind the products and more things that we offer. Yeah, that's very cool. I don't know that I've seen this specific design with the bento box and the modals that you can pop up that give more information directly on the page. It's an interesting, smart choice because rather than take people to a dedicated page, you keep them right here, show them what they want, and you give buttons to jump to the next section. Or you can keep scrolling down if this is not the right thing that you're looking for. Yeah, you want it to feel light. You want it to feel easy to kind of explore and engage and you're taking them on a journey for sure. You want to help them get to where they're going. But yeah, I think if we let people off the page, you know, it might be too early for them to have made their decision on what they're actually going after. So this is really just a bit more of a browse experience and it gives them a bit more of a chance to explore. YC Startup School is back. We're hand-selecting the most promising builders in the world and flying them out to San Francisco for July 25th and 26th to discuss the cutting edge of tech. Apply now for a spot. Okay, back to the video. Yeah, it's interesting. Another thing that stands out to me is how much animation you use in each of these cards. Talk a little bit about that and how you can do it without making it too overwhelming or distracting for people. Yeah, it's a difficult balance to get. I mean, this is where prototyping and experimentation matter. I remember this one where it's like we had too many lines at first and then we had too few lines at first and the ball was moving too fast. So you do have to really fine-tune and bring it in. One of the things, as pointed out earlier, is that when you're building a website like this, you are both directly and indirectly expressing your company's manifesto. One of the things that we are trying to express is the care that we put into the work that we do. Because if you see the care that goes here, then you're right to assume that we also put that care behind the scenes in the way that we move money, in the way that we protect a company's information. These are important details to express. It's also the idea of making the site feel a bit more alive. You know, that there's a presence here is something that I think the motion does really well. You are right though, if you go too far with it, it becomes distracting, it becomes annoying, and nobody really wants to engage with that. And so it's just delicate movements and really just like trying to fine-tune it and also thinking about the role that when somebody engages with it, that's where you bring certain things to life. You make sure that you're responding, and this gives the feedback to also register, like, "Okay, I can click on this." You might not have known that otherwise, so we try to engage in that way. This is where we're basically—these are kind of like the big important metrics that can help you understand again the scale of what we build. And you know, of course, there were versions where we could just put the numbers there. But what we intended to do with this is to just add a little bit of interest. It is loosely communicating the intention of these metrics. It's not really a data viz, of course, but it gives you a sense of like, "Okay, here we're talking about a global scale, here we're talking about uptime," and so you kind of get the sense of continuity here. But really it's just fun and it's just beautiful. And then it changes depending on what time of day, but if you want to go ahead and see what it would be like at night, you can come in and check it out yourself. It feels like we're entering an era now where websites can be fun again. Yeah, yeah. They were fun early on and then it got to a point where every SaaS website looked exactly the same and it's really cool to see you focused on things that are just fun for the sake of being fun. Yeah, I mean, I'm an AI hopeful that the creation of these tools can help us move so much faster and basically get to good almost instantaneously and almost for free. And now great, we can take that time to then push it to that next level and to create something that is really interesting and playful and beautiful. Yeah, I think what's interesting here that's maybe worth for anybody watching to pay attention to is that these aren't animations for animations' sake or interactivity for interactivity's sake. There's an intention and a purpose behind everything that you're doing. It's connecting to the specific message that you're trying to communicate with each of these numbers. And it's easy to look at it and go like, "Oh yeah, it's just like a fun kind of thing," and it is that, but it's not random. A lot of thought goes into that, I'm sure. Talk about some of the different things that you explored. Were people even against putting this in, thinking, "Well, what value does it have and every inch of the screen has to have directly measurable value?" Well, what's also interesting is it's this section that the site didn't launch with in December. We ended up launching in January and we were at a stage with this where like we had something good, like we had all four animations, but they didn't move quite the way we thought would feel really smooth and really intentional. The extra detail of how they transition from one to the next just felt a little cludgy and it wasn't quite as smooth as it could be. And it was a decision, a group decision, that we should wait and we should do it right. We could have said, "Well, let's just only do three," or "Let's just only do one," or maybe you don't make the move. And you know, for sure, what we don't want to get in the habit of is just pushing timelines out until the end of time. But this was a decision that it was worth it to get it done well and make it actually a kind of joyful experience that demonstrates that love and care and the technical ability. And you know, it was over the holidays and so pushing it another week was not going to be the end of the world. It was a really good thing in the long run about how it feels within the overall composition. So this area is a place where we're talking about our users but we created these images so it brings together Stripe, the brand, and the companies that we serve. Each one of these is custom-made and it's intended to express a bit of the brand that we're highlighting as well as the Stripe brand with the parallelogram. You could look at this and you could be like, "Oh yeah, great opportunity to use AI," as did we. We're like, "Great, we're going to use AI and it's going to be awesome and we're going to get these done in a day." Now, of course, the reality is building with AI is actually also something that requires the love and care and attention of the makers. And so there was definitely a process behind it. But these are really just meant to bring some interest and kind of again, as you're trying to express through visuals, a lot of people scroll really fast through the website, right? At least I do. I don't know about you. But when you're trying to get them to take a beat, almost like a song, it's like one of those key moments of punctuation. Where do we want folks to slow down and take a note and also when are we trying to express something? Like trying to communicate the type of brand that we work with, especially if you're not familiar with them. And now we've moved to a darker background. Is there something intentional behind that now? Well, this is an area that we're talking about our integrations and the way that our APIs work. And certainly there's a developer language. Not everything has to be in dark mode, but it is actually something we have done on the previous site as well and so we wanted to carry that forward. This is the area that we call "Squeezy Boy." Design is definitely more fun when you can give everything a fun name. But it is an area where we're kind of just showing some of the things that are comings and goings. This is our Black Friday Cyber Monday city that we had built, and our podcast "Cheeky Pint." So this will always be changing with different things that come through, but we wanted to once again have a little fun with how we portray these things, make it easier and also inviting to engage with. Certainly added more challenge to it. Like so for example, how do you get an image that when it's this size is going to make sense as much as it's going to make sense when it's this size? So this was tricky to build it right and so quite a bit of iteration on that, but I think the team found a clever way. It's amazing seeing the finished product here and everything is so polished and put together, but I think it's really easy to miss all the work and the iteration and the process that goes on behind the scenes there. I would love for you to take us through, show your work on all these different sections and all the iterations that you went through to ultimately end up at this point. Well, okay. We're starting here on the top where we have this wave. I've worked at Stripe now for five years and I cannot even tell you how many different variants of a wave or gradient or gradient wave that I have seen and been a part of. I think we were trying to find every possible solution. On this project, you know, we knew we wanted to bring a lot of color and joy to the experience. We knew that a wave or a gradient wave like we had before is a key way to do that and we enjoy that part of the brand. But which wave? How to do it? What's going to stand the test of time? What could be on there for six years potentially and give you a bit of joy when you visit, but not overdo it? What's super cool is that our wonderful eng team created this tool where we can experiment and get the right wave. The perfect wave. Do I want that much blur? No, not that much. Do I want more grain? No, not that much. Let's see, we want to also switch it up on the scale, how do we want it rotated, how thick do we want, how much texture do we want. The reality is all of those factors matter so much. Like what exactly is the color? Like that yellow isn't right, right? That's not what we ended up with. We want to find just the right color mix, just the right texture, just the right kind of movement. Having a tool like this was essential to make that happen. And then, you know, we first get it right and okay, we see how it is here and then becomes the building process. Like how do we actually build it so it's also performant and is going to operate really smoothly on anybody's computer? And I notice some buttons here, it looks like you can save the state. So do you create different variations of these and then kind of test them against each other? What was the process even of using this tool? Yeah, before we even got in that tool, we were even just trying to get centered on well what is it that we're actually trying to achieve with the wave. What are we talking about here? How vibrant do we want to be or do we prefer it muted? Some of these were really interesting, but is that the right feel for the page or not? This is one of our previous waves from one of our event sites, which was monochromatic but also really bold. Or do we want to be a little bit more diverse and more rainbow-like? And so we looked at each of these kinds of ways of thinking about it and then essentially had discussions about like well is it flat or is it an object with a form. And these great discussions help us tease out what we're actually trying to do. Of course, Patrick was heavily involved in the site as well and so we had many discussions with him like, "Does this wave feel right?" Because when you're creating a new homepage, it's almost like you're deciding what clothes you're going to wear for the next many years, so it's a big decision. It's amazing seeing the literally infinite variations that you could come up with here. I imagine that makes it harder to just make a final call. What was that process? Who decides and how do you decide, "Like this is the one that we're going with and move on from this," because you could probably spend forever debating waves. Yeah, that's very, very true. Usually, well for something like this, Patrick was definitely a part of the decision-making process. And so usually with the team we will look at quite a large range. Then we will down-select to a subset of those and say like, "Okay, these are the ones that we're comfortable with." Like we don't want to put in front of Patrick ones that we're definitely not comfortable with or wouldn't recommend. Although it's always helpful to show kind of like the process and what we did explore. But then we look at the ones that are likely a very good fit and then we'll have a conversation about what feels right for him. Luckily, he's also exceptionally decisive and has very good taste, and so we usually can use that as a conversation of like okay this one feels right. But I'll also admit, this is one of the blessings and the curse of the design process, that something that can feel great here on a board or in isolation all of a sudden feels very different when you actually put it in place. You feel it with the rest of the typography and the logos on there and the various other things and also just like sit with it for some time. So we definitely evolved it over time. Something that may have felt right at one point starts to feel just like it isn't going to stand the test of time and then we'll want to change it. At what point in the process do you go from looking at a bunch of different variations on this to actually get it coded up and working live in the page so that you can get a sense of what it actually feels like live? I mean I would say the goal is as quickly as possible to get it into a state that would be like the way a user would experience it. It is really hard to judge anything in isolation in a way that isn't like that user would be. That's what you're trying to do is just like put yourself in their shoes and then see what that would feel like. Sometimes we're just not there yet. Like it could take some time to code it up, and so we want to kind of start to down-select just so we know like should we code these five versions or these five versions. And so we would start perhaps with something that's more of just like a static visual and just get a sense, "Okay, we want to go over here or over there." But that's where that tool comes in handy because this allows us to essentially get many versions at once. And so we can play with them together. Yeah, and one of the other things that you might not get to experience just playing with it in frames is it comes behind the text there and so you have to change the text color and make sure that the text is readable and not distracting and it interacts with all these other elements on the page that are really important too. Right. You talked about a few different bento variations. I'm curious to see some more of those. Yeah, so this is the bento where we're trying to communicate a lot of different things that we do and what's the right way to communicate this. We looked at a ton of different things. What we're trying to figure out is what kind of user are you: are you a micro-entrepreneur with a small business or are you a large enterprise, are you interested in billing or are you interested in connect? These things. Do we want to show you them all in one screen? This might be an approach so we can get there faster because of course, on any website, the more you scroll down the less users get there, right? So what if we just squished it all in? Well, that's a lot, it's pretty type-heavy, and we're not doing a whole lot of showing versus telling. We looked at these kinds of sections. This is a little bit more similar to our original site that I showed you where you just kind of like pass through section by section. This felt like a little too scroll-y and telling. You kind of want to get to it and you want to get to it faster and you want to be able to get a quick sense of what we offer. Then we have here essentially an accordion. So you could press these things to then open them up and see what we have. This one we actually did take to user research and unsurprisingly it was not a quick way for people to really digest a lot at once because it requires effort, you know, even if it's not a lot of effort, right? But you want someone to tap on it or you want them to scroll over it. Otherwise, you're trying to introduce something to them like manage a marketplace end-to-end. You might know what that means or you might not, but would you be interested enough to click through? Most people just don't click tabs. Yeah, exactly. So that's why this didn't feel right and a more visual one was definitely a better choice. But there's a lot of different variants here in the way that we explored how to communicate this. Then again, you know, it just—you have to remember that when people are coming to this site, they're in more of a lean-back mode. They're in more of a browse mode. So the idea that you're going to put your most important messages a click away is going to be tough. And so that's where the bento kind of won at the end of the day because it was just so much more visual and I would say kinder to the users because it meant that they could be in that lean-back position. Talk a little bit about the process and maybe how you used AI in building this. Well, so yeah, let's start with the images and then there's other parts of the process there. But these were certainly a type of thing that lent themselves to AI. I think AI is really good at these pictures that seem, you know, super real. And so this is kind of what we're going for. Now it seems like it would be pretty simple to ask a, you know, an AI like FreePik or whatever like, "Give me a parallelogram inside this thing." But the reality is the nuance and the details really mattered to us. You couldn't just put something that sort of got there. It had to feel like it was at the same level of love and care as these other things. What was really kind of funny was the process here because one wouldn't have thought that bubbles would have taken so much of our attention, but they sure did. Here was a version that we had for a different user that is not portrayed on the site just right now. And you can kind of see like okay, yeah, it's an ice cube and the bubbles and you know, generally, what do you think? Looks okay? Yeah, looks real. Ship it. Well, this is the team's thoughts on the matter. Each one of these things is actually a critique of where it just doesn't feel quite real. The example from URBN is the—how well does this all feel like the right URBN layout? Now, it gets a lot of it right, but when you take a double look, her arm isn't quite right, he doesn't quite have a hand, you know, that shadow isn't exactly how it would be. And so the detail in each of these little nuances, like each pixel, basically needs the same amount of attention. I think what AI is helping us do is it's helping us speed up our process and certainly helping us explore more. We can look at 20 ideas in the time it normally would have taken to look at two. However, it doesn't replace craft, it doesn't replace taste, it doesn't replace the attention to detail to ensure that you're getting each of those things right. We're really excited about how it's sped up prototyping and experimentation. How should these accordions move? Should it replace this or should it push that? We can mock that up really fast. This saves engineers countless hours and designers as well as they're exploring different variety. It's also really great for user testing. If we want to be able to change all the words in here so that it feels one way for one user that we're talking about versus another, we can do that really easily with AI prototyping. I would say even during that time period, you know, what we were exploring feels old-fashioned a year ago versus how we explore today. How about like the role of designers? It seems like it's not just pushing pixels in frames in Figma. Talk about what the designers on your team are doing and the role that they played in actually building this. What designers at Stripe endeavor to do is to create things that feel like they push the status quo forward. And that kind of all harks back to the idea of what is the global progress that we can provide. We want to do that via design as well. We want to create things that are exceedingly easy to use, that are also really, really powerful and that can help people solve problems or help them build their business or help them accomplish what they're trying to do. But do so in a way that actually just brings a little joy to the day too. Because frankly that's part of progress as well—that a beautiful world is definitely a better world. I want to live in that one. Designers are trying to create things that are going to be really intentional for our users but also do so in a creative and thoughtful and differentiated way. I would say the tools that we're using are allowing for that now hopefully better and faster. As we talked about earlier, I think design AI can give us opportunity to raise the floor, essentially to create baseline products—maybe the seven out of ten—really quickly and easily. But then what do you do with that extra found time? Do you just ship that and just ship more sevens out of tens? Boy, I hope not. What instead we want to do is get the things that are very typical. Take a modal to accept somebody's email address to sign them up. Maybe you don't need to reinvent the wheel there, as long as that is a really good version of that, then we're good. But now can we spend the time to think about new paradigms of interaction? For example, people are using agents to build their businesses now. They're using agents to basically do everything under the sun. So what is your agent experience? How good is that? What is the user's experience going to be like when they are traversing the internet or their tools in that way? That's where I would say designers are putting more time now, thinking about those new paradigms of interaction and trying to think about what great user experience or agent experience looks like for tomorrow. We have incredible new tools that are available for everybody. And one of the trends obviously that we've been seeing a lot of is everybody within a company now having the ability to ship code and improve products and update the website and the core product that customers are using, which I think is really powerful. One of the questions that I think comes out of that, especially from a design perspective, is how do you keep it so there's this cohesive design language and it stays to the bar that is so important to you and the rest of the team without being directly in the flow of how new code gets written and shipped and going live on the site? Yeah, I'm really interested to see and be a part of the evolution of design systems in this way because design systems have always been a way to help a team scale. As an organization grows and you can no longer just know what the right thing is because there's ten of you seated around a table and you're all talking about every single thing you do. When you scale, it helps the organization make decisions together and ensure that there's coherence in the user's experience. With AI tools, I think that we're going to see a whole new wave where these systems are kind of managed by the AI tools and that can help us scale things a lot more quickly. For example, as I mentioned, I drew a sketch of what I wanted to see built and then I put that in the AI tool and then it uses the components from our design system to piece that together for me and get a rough version. Then I want to push it here and there and actually you know what, that component isn't really fit to purpose for what I'm trying to do here. This is where we're going to need to push from that. Then can the AI tool help me actually expand the design system and actually help to grow it for the use cases that we need? I think there's a lot that is going to be facilitated in terms of bringing more high-quality things at that baseline. But honestly, I mean this is what people have to choose to do. Because sometimes it's so easy when you put it in the AI tool and it spits back a thing and you're like, "Oh that was fast and that was easy and that's pretty good." You will feel it in yourself just being like, "Yeah, I guess that's enough." But the reality is don't be wooed by just how easy that was to achieve but instead ask yourself, "But is this really great? Have I really hit the mark? Is this really going to achieve the goal? Is this really going to feel like it's well-crafted and has intentionality behind it?" Sometimes that takes another rev and another turn. The way that our tools today can allow you to go back and tweak this little bit and tweak that little bit—essentially the merging of design and eng tools in one allows you a lot more ability to kind of customize that. You don't need to accept slop and you shouldn't accept slop. You should hunt for, fight for the right solution. What are some more principles like that that you try to instill in your team? I mean, I think a part of it is the reality—and I think we may have even talked about this last time we talked—the gravitational pull is to mediocrity. It is just so easy to accept "good enough." I think it really, really takes intentionality and you really have to think about what is the cost of accepting "good enough." In that one decision you're just like, "Well, it's just fine, let's just ship it, it achieves it, we've got too much to do." Is it like that data viz thing we were talking about before? It's pretty good, we could get the team on something else, you weigh all these other costs and you think about it and you end up making a decision to go for "good enough." But think about if you made that "good enough" decision every day, all day, every day. And then think about how many companies do that, right? How many companies really stand out to us as exquisite and exceptional and that really hold that bar up? That reminds you of just how hard it is to fight that fight of, "Well, if I let this slide, what else am I going to let slide and what is that composite going to be?" I think a lot of people make decisions based on, "Well, the team put so much effort in, I'm going to upset the team if I don't go forward." Well, how happy is the team going to be if the product is just meh? Fight the gravitational pull to mediocrity and do not leave "well enough" alone. Really look for the thing that's going to make a difference. I think the other thing that we talk a lot about internally and I think is really important for folks to consider when they're building products and experiences is what is the user going to experience at the end of the day. We get a little too caught up in our own world and we're too comfortable with it. So what I would always say is just realize it the way a user would: prototyping, not presenting. Don't try to communicate to the stakeholder why you did this and the tradeoff that you fought against and this and that. If you're there to talk about whether or not this is the right solution or not, then you should be asking, "Well, how is this going to feel at the end of the day for the person that's going to be experiencing this?" Without all of that knowledge, do I think that part is the right one? That makes a big, big difference. I would also say when we're exploring different products and what is actually shipworthy or not, you think about what the tradeoffs are. Is this going to negatively impact somebody's day? Is this going to be something that erodes trust? If you don't get this right, is this going to make it to the front page of the news? What are the tradeoffs when you're making a decision? A lot of the things that I'm talking about here are things that sound like I'm looking for perfection. I think that's also a dangerous pursuit. Because of course, your product is only as good as what's actually out there, what's shipped. It's not what we've been thinking about, it's not what we've been talking about, it's not our intention and what we're pursuing; it's what's actually available to the people that you're serving. And I think progress is probably more important than perfection in that case. A lot of times it's not out there until you actually can learn and understand whether or not it's effective, especially with AI tools and the way that you really don't know how people are going to use it and how it's going to respond and how they're going to evolve with it until you can bring it actually to market. So I think you have to have a kind of a rubric in place about what are the essential pieces to a successful product. I wouldn't call it an MVP, it's probably like an MVQP like Minimum Viable Quality Product. Because again, you don't want to lose trust by experimenting with something out in the world, but you certainly want to learn from their experience. It feels like something that's becoming even more important these days with all the advancements in AI is QA and testing. You talk about having the voice of the user and the perspective of the user in your head as you're designing. Part of what you do at Stripe, which you told us about last time, is "walking the store." And you actually go in and you test all of the products yourselves and you do that as part of a company culture and a requirement that everyone is expected to do that, which I think is really important. How has that changed more recently either within Stripe or certainly with all the changes with AI that have been happening? "Walking the store" is such an important part of building products and experiences because at the end of the day you're trying to understand what the user is going through. When you're building something it's so easy to think, "Oh yeah, we're in our product, we're in it all day. I'm thinking about my product all day." But what you're thinking about is that next feature that you launch. And it's really easy to lose sight of what's actually out there. It's kind of funny how you can create something and say, "Oh when we shipped it, we did all these bug bashes and it was great." But then you use it a month later, or six months later, and it's evolved. It's changed. And the reason why is because probably somebody else shipped something and somebody else shipped something. It's like in your home when you've updated the dining room but none of the other rooms and all of a sudden the light switches don't match and the colors are off and every other room now looks bad. You have to think about how that composite works together. For Stripe, for example, we have a number of different business areas. We have our money movement business, we have our revenue business, we have our payments business. These organizations need to focus and they need to stay true to their mission and what they're trying to get done. But if they don't think about how their product intersects with each other's and how a user might go from using subscriptions to payments to tax, then they're really missing the user experience. Walking the store helps people see the forest through the trees and see how all these things connect. Yes, we do—basically it's a part of the culture that everybody does this. We do have a program that we talked about last time, Essential Journeys, where we have a subset of these journeys kind of like on a scoreboard—are they red, yellow, green, and how good are they. But everybody should be exploring the store, different parts, imagining themselves as different types of users, experiencing different sides of it and trying to find the dead ends. We also do this every Friday where the founders do it in front of the whole company, where they're walking the store. One, this is to just instill how important this is and kind of raise awareness and also to help people think about how do you see. Not everybody has the same attention to different types of details. I might see something that you might not because of my discipline or my disposition or the user that I talked to earlier that week and the thing that I'm interested in. When we do these things together we see the value that different points of view bring. I really love to walk the store with an engineer in the room and a product leader in the room and a data scientist in the room because we're all looking at it very differently. We're all going to point out different things. And that is what gets us closer to really actually understanding what a user might feel. Well, Katie, this has been incredible. Thank you so much for coming and I feel like it's so rare to get an honest peek behind the scenes and show the process and all the steps that you had along the way. So really appreciate you sharing that with us. It was a lot of fun. I'm always happy to talk about behind the scenes. I think the blood, sweat, and tears and the effort that goes into it is something that's a part of the work but is often not talked about. I love hearing it from other designers and I'm always happy to talk about it myself. Thanks for having me on. Awesome. That's it for another Design Review. Thank you for joining us and we'll see you on the next episode.

Alain Lam - CFO of Xiaomi | Podcast | In Good Company | Norges Bank Investment Management

Norges Bank Investment Management(106.0K) ·40:20 ·3.6K views · 2026-04-22
Alain Lam (CFO, Xiaomi) Nicolai Tangen (CEO, Norges Bank Investment Management)
Xiaomi has evolved from a smartphone startup into a diversified global technology leader by leveraging "China speed" and a "Human x Car x Home" ecosystem to deliver high-quality innovation at "honest prices.
TL;DR Xiaomi has evolved from a smartphone startup into a diversified global technology leader by leveraging "China speed" and a "Human x Car x Home" ecosystem to deliver high-quality innovation at "honest prices." The "China Speed" Advantage: Xiaomi launched its first...

TL;DR

Xiaomi has evolved from a smartphone startup into a diversified global technology leader by leveraging "China speed" and a "Human x Car x Home" ecosystem to deliver high-quality innovation at "honest prices."

Strategic Evolution: From Smartphones to Electric Vehicles (EVs)

  • The "China Speed" Advantage: Xiaomi launched its first smartphone in 2011, just one year after the company's founding. It repeated this rapid development cycle with its first electric vehicle, the SU7, moving from project inception in 2021 to a 2024 launch.
      • Development Philosophy: Unlike competitors who produce multiple models, Xiaomi focused 10x the typical investment and 3,000 R&D staff on a single car to ensure immediate market leadership.
  • Vertical Integration: While the company previously outsourced smartphone manufacturing, it built its own large-scale, highly automated factory in Beijing for the EV business.
  • Market Traction: Demonstrating immense brand loyalty, Xiaomi sold 50,000 cars within 30 minutes of launch. Roughly 20% of buyers placed orders without a test drive, relying on the brand’s reputation for quality and fair pricing.
  • Global Expansion: Xiaomi currently ranks #3 in the European smartphone market. The company plans to begin exporting its EVs to Europe in the second half of 2025, supported by a dedicated EV research center in Munich.

The Integrated Ecosystem: Human x Car x Home

  • Ecosystem Synergy: Xiaomi manages a "Human x Car x Home" ecosystem that connects over one billion devices globally.
      • IoT Integration: Smart home devices (ACs, washing machines, smart locks) are designed to interact seamlessly. For example, unlocking a door can automatically trigger the home climate control.
      • The Car as Consumer Electronics: Alain Lam describes the EV as "the next generation of consumer electronics," emphasizing software-hardware integration and connectivity over traditional automotive drivetrain focus.
  • Customer Loyalty: The company maintains a user base of 180 million people in China who use Xiaomi smartphones daily, creating a built-in audience for its expanding product lines.

Advanced Manufacturing and Humanoid Robotics

  • Internal Application: Xiaomi has been developing humanoid robots for over five years. Unlike competitors targeting consumers, Xiaomi's current focus is using robots to enhance its own manufacturing efficiency.
      • Physical AI: The latest humanoid models feature human-sized hands with high degrees of freedom and advanced cooling systems for repetitive industrial tasks.
  • AI-Driven Production: The company uses AI for more than just chatbots; it is deeply embedded in the manufacturing process:
      • Material Simulation: AI was used to simulate over 100 formulas to find the optimal material for the EV's "gigapress" rear floor.
      • Quality Control: AI-powered X-ray systems have replaced human inspectors for detecting manufacturing defects.

Leadership and Innovation Culture

  • Lei Jun’s Product-First Vision: The founder and CEO is described by Lam as a "product person" who personally test-drove 150 different car models to understand market gaps. He required the entire management team to earn professional racing licenses to truly understand the automotive product.
  • AI Strategy: Xiaomi recently launched its own Large Language Model (LLM). Lam notes that while US and Chinese firms are the primary superpowers in AI, Xiaomi’s advantage is its massive repository of physical data from its connected devices.
  • Supply Chain Localization: Over the last 15 years, Xiaomi has shifted from relying on standardized foreign components to working with local partners to create highly customized, proprietary solutions.

Notable Quotes

"We want to build amazing products with honest prices to let everyone in the world enjoy a better life through innovative technology." — Alain Lam
"If you believe that a car, an EV, is going to be another piece of consumer electronics, that's something that we have a lot of experience in." — Alain Lam
"99% of the problems... there is already a solution out there, you just need to find it. And maybe with AI you can find it faster now." — Alain Lam (quoting Founder Lei Jun)
Hi everybody, and welcome to In Good Company. I'm Nicolai Tangen, the CEO of the Norwegian Sovereign Wealth Fund, and today, I'm in particularly good company with the CFO of Xiaomi, Alain Lam. Hello Nicolai, thanks for having me. Now Alain, Xiaomi's just an incredible company with phones, cars, humanoids, you know, we are the lucky owner of more than 1% of the company. So just tell us, what do you do and how did it all happen? Well thanks Nicolai, thanks for the question. And good to be here. So first of all, Xiaomi is still a very young company. We just turned 16... we were founded on April 6th, 2010. And since then we have done a lot, as you mentioned. But ultimately, I think we think of ourselves as a global technology company. Obviously we have smartphones, we have a lot of smart devices, plus recently we have smart electric vehicles, and we are into manufacturing. But underlying all of these is our technology, whether it is our operating system, our semiconductors, or our AI. I think those are the stuff that we built underneath all of our ecosystem, right? I think we built a pretty comprehensive what we call a Human x Car x Home ecosystem, ranging from all your personal devices to all your home devices and now with your mobility solutions. So that's where we are, but you know, we've achieved quite a bit over the last 15 years. Obviously last year we had over 450 billion RMB in terms of sales and we have achieved pretty good profitability. I'd say, it's just such an incredible story, so I'm not quite sure even where to start, but why don't we just go through it chronologically? The first product you had, you know, just how did it start? We actually started off building the operating system for smartphones, and that was in 2010 when we founded the company. We wanted to do an operating system on top of the Android ecosystem and then try to offer it to smartphones. But eventually we decided to get into the smartphone ourselves, right? And our first smartphone, which we call Xiaomi 1, we sold it at 1999 RMB in China. And that became a huge success because, I mean, as you remember back in the days, there were a lot of products out there that were low quality but high prices. How long did it take you to develop the telephone? So we launched our first smartphone in 2011, so one year after we've started. How is it possible to have such a short cycle time? Yeah, because at that time, I think the supply chain was obviously quite advanced in China. But the problem in the market at that time was there was a lot of expensive products with very low quality, right? And the philosophy today for the company is, can we do a smartphone that is much better quality and we can offer to users at much more affordable prices? And that's something that you see permeated in the history of our products, right? We try to offer very high quality solutions to our users at affordable prices. How do you think about your supply chain? How much of that is Chinese-sourced and how much is sourced outside? Well I mean, at the beginning, I think there was still a lot of reliance on foreign technologies. But over the last 15 years, for a smartphone, frankly, most of the content now is localized. You can find local solutions for that in most of our components. How much easier is it for you as a firm to be fast in product development when you can source everything locally? It is important because the local, frankly speaking, if you think about the foreign products, a lot of them are more standardized solutions off the shelf, right? Whereas the local partners are willing to work with you, developing products that are more customized to what we want, right? And as a result, it's not just faster, but it is also our ability to offer much more customized solutions that fit better into our smartphone, that fit better into what we want. And as a result, the go-to-market, we are able to offer products to our customers as something that they really want. So you are now at Model 17. You've aligned with the Apple kind of numbers. You had a setback in 16, what happened then? Well, I think there was such a big leap between 15 and 17 that we think that it's beyond one generation in terms of improvement from our Xiaomi 15 to our Xiaomi 17, and hence we name it Xiaomi 17. The innovation comes when you look at the smartphone—I don't have one with me right now unfortunately—but it offered a back panel, right? So instead of having a front panel like most smartphones do, we actually offered a back panel as well, so you can use both sides effectively. And that's something that has proven to be very groundbreaking, and a lot of our users like it. How important has the kind of the Internet of Things or your connectivity with home appliances been? It is important because I think in this world a lot of our users like intelligent solutions, right? So you know, think about air conditioners, think about washing machines, think about refrigerators. People, I mean, the technology has existed for 30, 50 years, but that hasn't changed for a long time, right? So what we offer to our users is smart solutions, right? Now you can upgrade your software over the air in your devices, right? As opposed to buying new machines, right? And a lot of our users value that, plus the fact is, can you make your whole lifestyle, whole ecosystem much smarter by connecting everything together so that they can do things for you? It's not just using your smartphone as a remote control, but smart intelligent solutions that understand who you are, they understand your behavior, and they can try to offer customized solutions for you every day. What other kind of things can you offer clients? Yeah, for example, a smart lock, right? Many people now install instead of a traditional lock, a smart lock, right? Not just can you look at who's outside of your door or who's delivering stuff to you, but you can offer special passwords for people to enter your apartment so that you don't leak your secrecy. But also, if you interconnect it with other devices at home, for example, when you open your smart lock, your AC will automatically switch on, right? That way you don't have to do anything extra, right? And those are the smart solutions that we like to think that our users need and, you know, obviously that became a very popular feature among our users. And then you decided to make a car. Correct. We decided to get into the car industry in 2021. And then we launched our first car in 2024. How is that possible? So a little bit less than three years, we launched our first car, we designed it, and then we built a factory as well, so China speed. Tell me about China speed. So, look, at the beginning, you know, the car industry to us is not that foreign. Xiaomi as a company has already invested in some of the EV manufacturers in China before, so we have an experience of seeing them develop and helping them develop over time. But then we decided to do it ourselves, and as you know, we've done a lot of due diligence in the industry the moment we decided to get into this industry. And I think the ultimate conclusion, I should say, is that if you believe that a car, an EV, right, is going to be another piece of consumer electronics, that's something that we have a lot of experience in. Whether it is managing the software-hardware integration or whether it is managing the supply chain, I think those are the stuff that we have experience working with so many consumer electronics products before. I mean obviously a car is a little bit of a different animal because you need to consider safety, but again I think the supply chain in China has been relatively well developed on the EV industry. So as we go into that, I think what we said was look, can we spend 10 times more investments to build the first car, right? I think that's also a very important philosophy as well is instead of going into all these different directions, making multiple cars, we decided to spend all of our effort on one single car. And it's a pretty fast car, right? Because now you set the record for the quickest round on Nürburgring last year. Yeah, look, I think as we look into this car industry and the EV industry as well, we think that there are certain things that are core to the EV technology that we want to own and develop ourselves, right? The rest we can have our suppliers work with us on that, but things like the electric motors is something that we spend real effort on. Things like how do you pack the battery, things like smart driving, assisted driving etc., those are the stuff that we spend real time on developing our proprietary technology. The rest of it, I think we can rely on other people to supply us with the industry solutions. How many people do you have in R&D? At the beginning, probably around 3,000 people to develop the first car, which is, as you know, in this industry probably 10 times more than other peers in terms of developing the first car. Because our chairman has the philosophy that, you know, we want to spend a lot of effort initially to catch up with our peers. Now, you sold 50,000 cars in 30 minutes. Yeah. Yeah, because look, I think obviously we are a very new entrant to the EV business, but obviously at the same time we already have a very established brand in China. We have a lot of loyal users of our products. We have over 180 million people in China that are using our smartphone every day. At the beginning, when we decided to develop a car, to develop an EV, a lot of our users said that if Xiaomi wants to make a car, we'll buy it, right? So 20-plus percent of our initial buyers haven't even test-driven the car, haven't even seen the car in real life, right? They just see us launching the car and they put an order in. So that's a testament to the loyal user base that we built up, that are loyal to the brand and they know that we are going to make the best products for them. How do you get people to love you? Well, I think it goes back to our philosophy, right? If you look at our mission, we want to build amazing products with honest prices to let everyone in the world enjoy a better life through innovative technology, right? It's a long sentence but, you know, we want to build amazing products, we want to offer these products at what we call honest prices to our users, and we want to do a global business, and we want to be innovative. So I think all those stuff are what users really value these days. Over time we've also made the coolest products, we make a lot of friends with our users, we stay engaged with our users, listen to the feedback, and try to change our products based on these feedbacks. So those are the stuff that we built a very good rapport with our users over time, and that's how they are loyal to our brand. They know that whenever they walk into our store, whenever they buy one of Xiaomi products, they are getting the best pricing possible. What are the similarities between making a phone and a car? Some differences, a lot of differences, obviously if you look at the phone industry, before 2024 we did not have a smartphone factory. So before that we were all outsourcing it to the Foxconns of the world, the BYDs of the world, and other suppliers to make the phones for us. We were more responsible for the design, for some of the basic technologies, and also for the branding and the marketing of the products. For the car business, you know, we also undertook the task of building an entire new factory in Beijing, right? And so that's a new experience for us. That's our first real large-scale factory that built our own car. So that's the difference, right? I think that's the one key difference between how we entered into the car business versus how we entered the smartphone business. But there are a lot of similarities because increasingly the software drives the hardware, right? In the EV just like they are in the smartphone. And a lot of it is, you know, in smartphones we stack up a lot of chipsets into a very small form factor. In the car it's similar, we have to stack up a lot of components into a form factor. But increasingly more important, especially in the EV world, the software becomes very important—how do you manage battery power, how do you manage the software to drive the car in a safe manner? I think those are very important similarities with the smartphone business. Obviously managing the supply chain is also very important. We have a lot of suppliers that are the same between the smartphone business as well as the EV business and that's why there's a lot of cross-collaboration. I think people trust the engineers of Xiaomi that we can build something that is world-class and that is very popular with the users. How far behind is Europe when it comes to EVs now? Look, we still look up to a lot of the OEMs, the traditional OEMs. We still think that there are a lot of things to learn from them—the way that they build the brand, the way that they build the drivetrain, the way that they give people a real good driving experience. Those are the stuff that we still look up to a lot of the European OEMs. What we think that they are lacking is, number one, the smartness of the car. I think they are trying to implement a lot of the smart features into the car right now, but I think in that sense they are probably behind what we are seeing in China right now. And then the second is kind of the whole NEV, the new energy vehicle development, right? I think that a lot of the focus was on electric at that time, but really I think when we look at our EV we call it the smart electric vehicle, right? And I think that some OEMs may look at electric too focused, but less focus on smart, right? I think that that's one thing that the users right now really want. Especially given the Xiaomi advantage, it's how do I link it with all of our ecosystem products? How does it work better with my smartphone? How does it work better with my IoT devices? You know, things like that, so that I can create not just an EV but also a smart life ecosystem beyond the EV. And how long do you think it will take the Western brands to catch up on that particular element? I think a lot of people are learning, right? I think a lot of people are studying how the Chinese EV development has become so advanced. We have obviously received quite a lot of European OEMs who've come to visit us, who want to have experience sharing with us. Not just we sharing with them but they also share with us, you know? It depends on how serious they are, and I think you already see a couple of the European collaborations with the Chinese players as well, right? So I think that that's going to be helpful in driving the industry forward. Now I think it was the CEO of Ford who used your SU7 for a little while, what did he say about it? He said he couldn't get rid of it after six months of driving it. Because he loved it too much? Apparently. I haven't spoken to him in person, but apparently based on what he's been saying in public, he loves our car. How is the EV market going to develop going forward? Look, I think the EV penetration will continue to go higher. In China, new car sales are already 50, 60 percent new energy vehicle, right? So that's very high already. But if you look at a lot of the global market, the new EV market is still quite low in terms of penetration. So I think that as the infrastructure continues to develop, as people appreciate the smartness and the drivability of these new electric vehicles, the penetration will go up. So when you see European and American producers actually pulling back their EV efforts, what do you think about that? Look, I think obviously there is a lot of investment that need to go in, especially for many of the legacy brands because they need to retool the whole systems to get NEV done. So, you know, a lot of them are probably not pushing that agenda as forward. But honestly speaking, we don't think that they... we think that the NEV is going to continue to penetrate. Whether the Chinese players are coming in or whether the European and American players are developing them themselves, or also the Japanese and the Koreans, so I actually think that the trend will be there longer term, right? There are obviously maybe hiccups along the way, but the whole industry will move forward in this direction. Sometimes there's a major breakthrough in terms of smartness of the vehicles and also the infrastructure. I think there are a lot of places whereby the infrastructure is probably not as well developed in other markets for the EV market to take off. Let's move on to robotics and humanoids, what are you doing there? I've just seen your latest models and it's pretty mind-boggling. Yeah, look, we've been doing robotics for five or six years now. That's an investment that we started making back in 2019, 2020. But then I think we are beginning to see the inflection point in this market. A combination of the hardware getting better as well as the software getting better, i.e. AI, you know that is driving the brain of the robot. The way that Xiaomi internally is thinking about it is we are making these humanoid robots to enhance our own manufacturing capability and efficiency. We haven't launched any 2C robot. So all the robots that we are developing or the humanoid robots we are developing right now are used in our own manufacturing scenario. I think the video already showed that there were two robots that are working consecutively for three hours with a very low margin of error. But still I think that's a long way to go because as you know, I'm sure that we'll move to AI at some point in this conversation, right? The AI right now is mostly focusing on learning from text data and from voice data that we have accumulated for a long, long time. But then the physical data are still quite a way behind in terms of the collection and the interpretation. So I do think that when you develop the large language model for physical AI, right now what we are still lacking is a lot of data that don't exist, right? And so you have to simulate it, you have to find out the use cases and find the corner cases etc., but the text and the voice are quite well developed at this point. Can you tell us about the improvement in the grip functionality in your latest model? Yeah, look, I think if you look at the video that shows... the hand is much smaller than the other versions. The hand is very similar to a human hand size now, right? And also the degree of freedom that it offered. I think one of the biggest challenges with the hand is how many degrees of freedom do you have in the hand. And then the second problem that we are trying to solve is the cooling, right? It's like a smartphone, it's like a car. When you use the machines for too long, they get heated, right? And once it gets heated, the consumer electronics rule would say that the performance will degrade, right? So what we're trying to do also is introduce a cooling system so that you can do some heat management of the hand as well so that it doesn't get overheated while you are doing the work repetitively. When do you think you'll have this for consumers? That's not the focus of Xiaomi right now to be honest, because we actually think that there is a big space for us. Ultimately we do hardware, right? We do a lot of hardware and there's a lot of manufacturing involved, right? So we want these humanoid robots to be able to improve our manufacturing efficiency first. Maybe to recreate our entire manufacturing processes, it could be, but that's already a huge market. So right now we're not thinking about doing any 2C robot at this point in time. Who do you think will win this race? It's hard to say, but suffice to say that if you look at this market right now in terms of innovation, in terms of entrepreneurship, in terms of technology leadership, there are two big superpowers in the world, right? The US and China. So the winner will come from one of these two places, but I don't know where yet. What's next? Well, I think we talk a lot about what we've been doing. I think that the next thing for us is to continue to develop our AI capabilities because I think that AI is going to be very important for the next wave of innovations and it'll impact all of our businesses. How do you use it today in the company? Well I mean, I think the simple things are coding, right? So a lot of our software engineers now use bit-coding. Second one is, can we improve our sales efficiency? Can we predict sales better, right? i.e. do I even need to hire an experienced sales store manager or I can use AI to predict what kind of sales should each store generate every day? Marketing materials, right? We can use AI to generate the first few drafts. And very importantly, even in the manufacturing process we've been using AI a lot, right? When we talk about our car, we have a big gigapress, right, that does the rear floor for the car. In terms of the material we use for that rear floor, we simulated over 100-plus formulas. We used AI to predict the performance of each of these formulas and ultimately we picked two to be the material for that rear floor that we do for the gigapress, right? In terms of each piece that we manufactured, they went through an X-ray room where they take a lot of pictures and they use AI to look into that to see whether there are defects in the manufacturing process, right? So you don't use humans to do it anymore, you just use AI to take pictures very quickly. They can diagnose whether this piece is good or not, and then you can use AI in terms of material generations and a lot of those things. So we are using AI in a lot of different areas, but there is more to do going forward. You've also developed your own language model. Yes, we just launched it two weeks ago. So we launched our own large language model two weeks ago and it has performed pretty well. I mean if you look at public scoring systems like openrouter.com, you can see that we've been ranked very highly on that table. So we are very glad that we have a large language model that people like and people use. And at much lower costs than the likes of Claude? Well I mean, these costs are never publicly disclosed information, so I don't quite know the cost of it, but our price that we offered is lower than Claude but much higher than the other models from China that are being offered right now. What are the cost advantages you have when it comes to, you know, the utilization of these models, giving lower energy costs, simpler model structures and so on? We did try to innovate a bit on the infrastructure of the model. So obviously we have the benefit of seeing how other people do it before and then we try to optimize our path. So in terms of infrastructure we have done some innovations, although we didn't talk too much publicly about that. Number two is if you think about the model development right now I think there's still a lot of way to go. The good thing for Xiaomi in a way is I already have a big ecosystem that we can use to test, we can collect data from those user behaviors, and we can refine our model. Last thing is obviously as you know that we put it as an open source model, so we also are trying to invite the best minds globally to help improve that model as a result, right? Because ultimately I think if you look at whether it's our car or our smartphone, we want to invite other people to join us together so that we can help improve the ecosystem together as well. So let's say 5, 10 years from now when you have integrated all your products with the latest AI models and so on, what's the vision of the world? What is it going to look like? As we think about how AI is going to change the world and how we fit into our own ecosystem, we think about it more in the physical AI world, right? How do we get AI into our phone, how do we get AI into our smart appliances, how do we get AI into our car, and then how to interconnect these things to make users' lives more efficient, much better? I think that's how we see this, right? Obviously other people are using AI to serve the coder community, and other people are trying to use AI to make very useful chatbots for the users so that they can manage their lives better. But for us, for Xiaomi, because we have over a billion connected devices globally and we have over 800 million smartphone users globally, how do we interconnect these devices so that we'll enhance people's productivity in the physical AI world? I think that's something that we are looking at. I have to ask you a question about the founder, Lei Jun, who's kind of considered a mix of Bill Gates, Steve Jobs, and Elon Musk. How is it to work with him? Well, Mr. Lei himself is an entrepreneur, a serial entrepreneur, but he's also a product person, right? I think if you look at when we got into the car industry, we decided that he should be the CEO of the car business in order to take it forward. And he will... I think you've seen other interviews of him where he personally has driven 150 different models of cars himself, and taken detailed notes so that he understands what's on offer in the market and how can he improve on it. I can assure you that you can find very few CEOs that have this real dedication to start off with this car business, right? He makes us, like all the management team...
