The election is going to turn basically on like whatever's happening in the economy and then it's going to be whatever is like the viral meme panic that's happening like that day. And so and you could say like is this good or bad, I don't know. Is this better or worse than television, I don't know. By the way, is this better or worse than the days of radio or the days of—I don't know, the printed revolution that followed the arrival of Gutenberg, the printing press? Is this better or worse than the days in which you know we were all reciting epic poetry around the campfire as our form of media? I don't know. You could have like a big value discussion about you know which is better or worse. I have an argument I will make that this notwithstanding all the craziness this is better, but like this is the nature of the world we live in. And look—put it this way—this is we're now on autopilot. Like the shape of the media determines the behavior basically from here on out. And until there's something that fundamentally displaces social media, like this is the world we live in. Hence the need to monitor the situation. Amen. And is the reason why you think it's better is because even though there's more noise there's more signal and as kind of a collective global brain we get closer to the truth or? So I would say this. We get—so a couple things. Yeah. So one is look, I would say there you could say this, there are more truth and more lies. Right? I would say both of the following statements are true: which is there's more truth on social media than we had in previous forms of media and there are probably—probably—I mean old media had a lot of lies by the way, so it's hard to say there are more lies, but let's just say there are plenty of lies in social media. In fact, one of the native forms of entertainment of social media is trying to spot the ops. Right? By the way, maybe I should pitch you a show for MTS which is you know maybe every day we should do an hour on "let's spot the ops." Right? Like what, you know, which ones are real and organic and which ones are staged bullshit you know with somebody getting paid behind the scenes. And so, you know, and everybody of course focuses on the downside and so they focus on the fact that there's all this—all these ops and bullshit which is certainly true. But there's also just like the ability for somebody to tell the truth is like much more direct and clear than it was before right? Because of—because of, you know, the collapse of the gatekeepers. And so I think both of those are true. So I think that's true. And then but the slightly different argument that I would make is that previous forms of media were really good at generating physical violence. And so the—I mean there's many many examples of this in history, but the Spanish Civil War which was kind of the the war that kind of pre-forecasted everything that was going to all the sort of tragedy of the 20th century that followed. The Spanish Civil War was kind of basically the test bed for both communism and nazism in the modern world. And you know its native media form was posters. And so you literally—it was like the idea—the ability to do color printed color propaganda posters was like the new media technology at that time right before radio. And the Spanish Civil War was like super violent and then radio was the technology that led to—you know radio was the Nazi regime's native media format. Radio was the way that basically the the fascist regimes got established as well as by the way—as well as FDR who was primarily a radio phenomenon, you know kind of the rise of modern liberalism. And then television, you know, was the native medium of like just—you know television kind of kicked in starting around World War II, so it played a role, but it then you know television had a huge role to play in the Vietnam War and then in all of the violent riots of the '60s and '70s. You know, which were kind of, you know, super crazy both, you know, across the US and Europe. And so, you know, television has a long history of leading to downstream violence. The fact that like—the fact that like political violence is at like an all-time low in Western society is something that people really don't talk about. Because it feels like we must be in this like super politically violent world because everybody's so angry about politics all the time, but like measured political violence is at like an all-time low. And so my theory is the ability to directly participate in online virtual combat is shunting away a lot of the energy that in the past would have would have translated to street violence. Right? And so it's sort of this thing is if the virtual world can become arbitrarily rhetorically violent without people actually getting physically hurt and that gives people a way to exercise all of their anger and rage over politics and to be able to attack, you know, the hated enemy without having it actually be something that translates to physical violence. And so so yeah, my theory is it's like—I don't know, social media wars up, you know, physical violence down. Nobody wants to hear that because everybody's so mad all the time about what's happening online, but I think that character—like if you're sitting at home scrolling social media getting mad, like at least you're not out on the street like hurting people. So that's my—that's my moral defense of the whole thing. Well, it's fascinating because there's kind of a different kind of ideological sorting happening because people—you say things like, "Hey, you know, now they can't even talk to their own families because they've sort of found their tribe online that agrees with them." And you know, it's no longer the people they—you know it's no longer their families and back in the day you didn't have as much—sort of the logic goes—as much political polarization of the idea that I can't even talk to someone not from the same side. Part of that perhaps is because the internet allows them to find their tribe so you know so strongly and so clearly that they're able to you know have an exclusionary tribe as well. But I guess one response to the political polarization argument is that, "Hey, at least it's not going to leading to physical combat." What might you say more broadly—is it just overstated in general because people also have this idea of filter bubbles too, but of course in some ways it's the opposite in the sense that the internet exposes you to ideas that you wouldn't have seen otherwise. Right. Yeah, so I think people just have a really rose-colored glasses view of the past. The past gets sanded off. Like in part because we only interpret the past—you know number one is we weren't there and then the modern interpreters of the past, you know, are often quite honestly not telling us the truth about what happened because they have their own modern kind of gloss or spin they want to put on it. And so you—but just the edges of the past get sanded off in retrospect. But it's just like—it's really significant. Like if you just—the easiest thing to do is go on YouTube and watch old episodes of a TV show called *All in the Family*. Which was a—which was a very a very famous, successful TV show when I was a kid. And *All in the Family* was a microcosm, a fictional microcosm of basically the culture wars, political wars of the late '60s into the 1970s. And it was—you know famously this character Archie Bunker. Who sort of represented, you know, as this white guy and he basically represent—a great brilliant actor named Carroll O'Connor played him. And he basically played a retrograde basically Republican, you know, Nixon-supporting Republican in this would be in like I don't know 1972 or something or '74. Who just basically was—was painted in the show as like the most backward, like basically archaic, you know, knuckle-dragging, racist, sexist, just like the worst possible person in the world. And then there was his son-in-law played by a young Rob Reiner, who, Archie Bunker nicknamed "Meathead," which was the character became known as. And Meathead was basically just the arch-progressive liberal. Right. And you know, sort of like—you know think I don't know young Gavin Newsom or something right? Where just like he had just like the exact politically correct, approved view of the time and so he's like you know super at the you know the equivalent then of super woke, super progressive, you know, super enlightened on on all these issues. And the conceit of the show, you know, and the guy who created the show as this famous television producer named Norman Lear. And the conceit of the show was supposed to be a moral lesson of Meathead giving basically moral lectures to Archie Bunker every week to try to get Archie Bunker to stop being such a backward-looking like 1950s retrograde basically racist sexist guy and come into the modern world. And of course what happened was Carroll O'Connor—number one the actor was just like super funny—but the other was the political environment was super heated at that point. And of course it turned out Archie Bunker ended up being the star of the show. And so the show ended up being the opposite of kind of what it was intended to be. That happened later in the '80s, that happened with a show called *Family Ties*. Which was again—it was a very similar thing which it was these two parents, baby boomer parents in the '80s and they were like super, you know, it was very left-wing producers who made the show and the characters were like super proper left-wing progressives. And then there was this character in the show, Michael J. Fox, their son, who is this teenage kid, and he played the—I forget the name I'm blanking on the name of the character but he played the son. And the son basically was a Reagan Republican as a teenager, right? And literally the son would like wear like sport jackets and ties to school and carry a briefcase. And they kind of wanted to make him into like a stereotype of like basically a you know sort of a weird Republican, you know, kind of weirdo. And again the same sort of weird thing happened which is actually the Michael J. Fox character became—oh, Alex Keaton was Alex was the character's name. Alex P. Keaton. He essentially became the star of the show. And so I bring up both of those shows because you can see in those shows, you can see the culture wars of the '70s which were actually like super intense. You can see the culture wars of the '80s which were super intense. You go back before that and you have the Vietnam War and the all the campus protests and the civil rights movement and all the riots and all the forced everything, you know, dropping the 101st Airborne into, you know, rural South to you know basically desegregate the schools at literally at gunpoint. You go back before that you have the Cold War, you know, you have the Red Scare, you know, what's now known as the Red Scare but you had, you know, communists all through the US government and you had all kinds of freak-outs about that. You had the risk of nuclear annihilation. You go back before that you had World War II which was like this—just like incredible global orgy of violence. You go back before that you had the Great Depression and the you know New Deal which was hugely controversial. You go back before that you had World War I. And then you know—if you keep going back it's like you have the Civil War and then you have the Napoleonic Wars and then you've got the French Revolution and you know you go back further than that you've got the you know the democratic revolutions all through Europe and then you've got the you know the Gutenberg printing press kicking off all kinds of violence, you know, throughout Europe in the 1600s. And so I just think yeah—the history of Western civilization, American and European society, is just like constant strife and warfare to like an absurd degree over this entire period. And we go back and we study the history and we're just kind of like, "Wow, like those people were like really really really violent. Like really crazy and really violent." And boy did they really like killing. It was not that long ago, and I don't think it's anybody alive now, but it was not that long ago that like grown men—like in serious positions of power and authority in the US—if they got mad at each other, they would literally have a physical duel. Right? Like so, you know, you and I—you and I start going at it on social media and we get to a point where we get really mad and I challenge you to a duel and like we show up in a fucking field in a fucking park, you know, at like in the morning with guns, right, and we like square off and march off and we turn around and we try to like literally shoot each other to death as like a thing that they did and that was like a normal part of life. Wait Marc, I thought the past was all matriarchies and everyone just so peaceful and— Oh, everybody was so wonderful. Everybody got along. Everybody, yes, until social media everybody thought the same thing. Yes, exactly. And everybody was all, yes, everybody was all calm and happy all the time. And so look, the way of the world is conflict. I mean just historically the way of the world is conflict. And anyway, not to beat it to death, but the you know there was plenty of conflict in the past. There were plenty of heated disagreements in the future shape of society. There were plenty of political wars. There were plenty of violent wars. By the way, just politics itself was much more violent. Like if you go back and you read about political movements a hundred years ago, like they would literally form up in the street and like fucking try to kill each other. Like it was like a common thing. Strikes. I mean just even the history of the labor movement is full of this, like strikes became super violent and, you know, management would literally—you know, the strikers would try to attack their plants and the management literally would have like machine guns and they'd be like machine gunning their striking workers. Crazy, crazy stuff. And so like we just—we live in a much crazier world than we want to give ourselves credit for and the idea that like the current level of conflict is something new is just not true. And just to that end too, when people say, "Oh, ever since 2008" or you know, "smartphone" sort of, you know, propagating that it's led to increased depression or things like that... Is that also another example of, "Hey, we were underestimating how depressed people were prior and it's kind of a measurement thing," or is it, "Oh, but lots of other things happened in 2008 too, like you know, political extremism and other things that that make people more depressed." How do you engage in that topic? Well, so the funny thing is different Western countries show different data on the quote-unquote rise in depression. And basically it turns out it correlates to social benefits programs. And so basically if you get paid or if you get like special dispensation in school for having psychological issues, all of a sudden you see a massive spike in those psychological issues. The countries that don't have that—the other Western countries that don't have that—don't show the same results. And so I actually think that's a statistical artifact of literally like government benefits programs. So like I don't even—yeah I would say I don't even I don't even I don't even concede the you know concede the premise of the question, but that's a longer debate. I would just say in general like I guess what to say? I am prepared to say that basically—call it since I don't know, 2014 or something—I'm prepared to say like it does feel like things have intensified, right? It does feel like things are like wilder, you know, than they were before that. I think the most that I would be willing to say though is I think we might have lived through an era of what you might call suppressed volatility. And call that maybe from like the end of the Cold War through 2014, where basically it just looked like the West had won everything. And then also it was sort of—by the way, it was sort of the peak heyday of centralized media. So it was if you just—like if you basically track—if you track like how distributed is media versus how centralized is media, like centralized media sort of peaked somewhere around 1970. Where, you know, literally like all the newspapers in every city consolidated down to like a single newspaper and then you ended up with only three television networks and all the radio stations—independent radio stations—got bought up. Let me give you an example. So historically, like if you go back to the days of the American Revolution, it was common in—in even like mid-sized cities, you would have like 10 or 15 different newspapers. And each newspaper would represent like a different political point of view. And then the newspapers would have these like furious, you know—and they'd just like—just like you would do today online, you—they would have these furious constant political fighting and accusations back and forth and arguments. And of course the newspaper owners would be trying to egg all that stuff on because it sold papers. But it was like very common to have like many newspapers per city that would all fight with each other. And then the economics of centralized media meant that those all rolled up into—into a single newspaper. And then that single newspaper, you know, at least for a while tried to be non-partisan and tried to be even-handed because it was trying to appeal to everybody. And then—and so—and again same thing of just like, you know, like when I was a kid, it was three television networks that were all basically the same. They were all trying very hard to be even-handed at least, you know, kind of as best they could. There were like three national news magazines, you know, there was a small number of national radio networks, you know, there were two, you know, news wire services. And so I—like I will concede—like I think we lived through a period of suppressed volatility as a consequence of the fact that we had overly centralized media. I think if you go back before that—like if you go back to before that, if you go back to like the 1930s and before—like media was much more fragmented. Much more like it is today. And then if you—like I said—if you go all the way back like 200, 250 years ago, it was like extremely fragmented. Like if you read the accounts of like Ben Franklin as a newspaper publisher and like the stuff that he got up with his newspaper... So okay, great story. So Ben Franklin ran one of the newspapers in Philadelphia originally as a printer. Okay, so what would happen was Ben Franklin wants to become—he wants to become a commercial printer, he buys a printing press, he advertises whatever, he tries to get like all the local business to print pamphlets on the printing press. But he doesn't have enough business for the printing press and so he starts a newspaper to have something to print. And so he starts this newspaper. There's like 15 other newspapers in Philadelphia at the time, but he's got to fill the newspaper with something. And so—and he's Ben Franklin, he's very creative—and so he creates basically sock puppets. He does basically ops, he does alts—alts, he does alts, yeah, yeah, exactly, alts—and they're all under pseudonyms. And so he does—I forget all the names now—but he had all these different names for them. And I think at one point in his own newspaper he had like 15 different alts going at it. And he—he wrote them all but under different names and they all had like very different points of view and then he would basically set them off to have these like raging arguments with each other in his newspaper and people would buy his—people would buy his newspaper to cover this. And then that—that culminated in the election of 1800, which was the famous election of Thomas Jefferson versus John Adams. Which was sort of this newspaper-centric election which was characterized by a level of slander—like the degree to which partisans of Thomas Jefferson and John Adams attacked each other in the 1800 election is like way beyond even what we see today on social media. Like they accused each other of the most horrible shit you can possibly imagine, and it became like this giant scandal. And so like this is not the—the idea that there's like whatever rhetorical combat or like that there's like this virtual environment in which there's lots of points of view and there's lots of, you know, again both lots of truth, lots of lies... like this is not new. In fact this—this is the natural order of things. We just lived through a period of artificial volatility and artificial suppression of volatility with media becoming overcentralized. And—and obviously those days are over. I want to return back to what we were talking about, the current thing and how that's accelerated over time. We previously had a conversation a while ago where we were talking about sort of the attributes of a current thing and I remember you had this one theory that it can't be something too believable, it has to be almost surreal. I'm curious if we just talk kind of what are the attributes of something that becomes sort of the current thing? Or sort of the main topic that people can't stop quote-tweeting, sharing, etc.? Yeah, so again it's kind of this thing you kind of reverse engineer it. It's like okay, to be a current thing it has to—if, you know, again to be this thing of "if it's on the internet it's a viral internet meme kind of outrage cycle"... So it—it has to be something that like—it has to be something that activates outrage. Like it has to be something that activates the emotional kind of valence of—you have to get the emotional reaction off of it. Right. And so you know it's just like—by the way this is just like also the history of the press in general. It's just like whenever you, you know, I always say the news is called the news, not the importance, right? Like you can imagine a version of the news that's called the importance and the newspaper is like, "Here are all the important things that are happening today." Right. And of course nobody would buy it because nobody cares. Because people want the news. They want the news thing, right? They want—or you know—or they want—they want the hot thing, they want the exciting thing, they want the—they want the outrageous thing. And so the internet version of that is—right, they want—they want the outrageous meme. And so so it's got to be something that provokes. And you know—and there's something about this—you know this is other people made this observation—but you know it's like if—you know you kind of the ultimate male-coded version of this is professional wrestling, right? The ultimate female-coded version of this is *Real Housewives*. Like you know reality TV, like you know or I don't know *The Apprentice* maybe somewhere in the middle. We—it's just like reality TV—like there has to—there has to be sparks. Like it has to generate a level of emotional intensity driven by some level of outrage. And then I think it has to be the kind of thing where you can have tribes that form up and square off against each other. Right? So so you know there's a little bit like—like *West Side Story* dynamic to it or something right? Where it's just like okay you can imagine there's something that's worth arguing over. Where there's—you know—where there's a real argument to be had. Like there—you know—there's some underlying thing that actually like really matters. Maybe—maybe this event doesn't matter as much, but like the actual underlying issue matters a lot. And then it's—it's going to—it's going to activate this tribal effect where you're going to be able to basically—you're going to become part of what they call a moral tribe. The people on the other side are going to become part of a moral tribe and then you're going to kind of go to war with each other. You're—you're going to have a rumble. What they would have said in the old days. You're going to have a rumble. A rumble in the streets, except you're not going to have a rumble in the streets, you're going to have a rumble online, right? You're going to go at it. And so it has to lend itself to that kind of basically tribal formation and—and—and kind of that beating back and forth. And of course you—you the tribes then attack each other, you know, on the issues, but then they also attack each other's values, and they accuse each other of being horrible people. And then when it gets really into it, they start to dox each other and try to get each other fired. Right, and it all becomes, you know, very exciting. And so so so it's got to—it's got to have the—basically it's got to have the formation of—of a moral tribe effect. In the sociological psychological lens on this they call moral tribes and then there's this concept right of moral panic. It's got to lead to a moral panic. It's got to lead to this kind of thing of "this is indicative of how society is going to shit," right, in—in like some important way. And by the way, this is why you can have like many, many, many, many different examples of current things that end up revolving back around the same handful of like underlying political or moral questions, right? Because it's really—right, it's really not about the, you know, what happened, you know, Jussie Smollett. I just watched this new—there's this new Keanu Reeves movie on Apple TV where Keanu Reeves plays a movie star who gets canceled. And his crisis lawyer played by Jonah Hill—it turns out during the course of the show—I'll just spoil it—at the course of the movie it turns out his crisis lawyer set up the Jussie Smollett was an op, it was a produced op. So in the movie world the lawyer came up with the idea that there would be a black celebrity who would get attacked in the street by white racists and that would be like a viral social media outrage thing. And then—and then literally the lawyer says, "Well then of course we went on cast and Jussie Smollett had the best pitch for why he should be—he should be the black celebrity." And then—and then there was one of his underlings he's—he's still mad at because the underling was tasked with the assignment of going out and hiring, you know, the attackers. But you know this is in the heyday of diversity and so he hired two black guys to be the attackers. Right and he's like all confused because he's like, "Am I supposed to hire black people not hire black people?" and it's like, "Well not in this specific case, you weren't supposed to hire black people to attack Jussie Smollett." Anyway, so the Jussie Smollett thing is fresh in my mind. But it was a great example which is like what happened on the street in Chicago that night during the whatever whatever blizzard cyclone thing it was? Like kind of matters, kind of doesn't matter. Who ever really knows? Who knows whatever we've got weird grainy video whatever people say different things. I mean I think we now know what happened but like in these events like what actually happened on the street who the hell knows? But the fight—you see what I'm saying? Like the moral tribes that were able to form up on, you know, one side where people are like, "Of course this is what, you know, this is what happens to people all the time." And the people on the other side who were like, "Of course this is all fake and bullshit." Right, you had this—you had this chance for these moral tribes to—to pair up and fight. And so I think it basically—I think it basically has to—I think it basically has to have that. Yeah, and sometimes it's very serious and stakes are very high and sometimes it's just an episode that's so, you know, funny and the astronomer CEO HR as a—as an example. Okay, so this—okay, this very important point. So very important point. So I would say two things. So one is, the truth or falsity of the actual event doesn't seem to matter at all. And then I think this is—I think this actually throws people. Because you start to think these are all fake. And they're not all fake. Like actual—actual bad things happen. Right? Actual bad things happen and it's—it's actually like fully legitimate to get outraged about those things. But also people are going to get outraged about a lot of things that actually didn't happen. And so so so for the purpose of the discussion of the media—again this is McLuhan thing, "if it's on the internet it's a viral social media outrage cycle." Like whatever are the facts of the thing, it doesn't really matter. In fact it's probably best—your point—it's probably best if it's actually hard to tell what actually happened. Because that makes it something that you can more easily argue about. And so that's one thing. And then to the point that you just made, I really agree with that also, which is the degree of importance of the actual issue doesn't matter that much. Right? And so if it—so if something happens to a single person, you can—and then something happens to 10,000 people, you can have the same level of outrage in—in each case. Like the outrage doesn't scale with the number of people affected. Right? In a lot—in a lot of ways maybe the outrage is even higher when it's a specific person because it—because then it becomes personalized. Right. And then you—and then you get in the old—what is it the old Stalin thing which is "a single death of the tragedy a million deaths is a statistic." Like maybe it's even the small-scale horrible things that happen that are the most emotionally intense because they're the things that you really—you're going to—you're going to react to a person—you know kind of—kind of the most intensely. And so so—and I think that's the thing if you're doing the meta observation of this. It's like okay you're just going to—you basically—you just have to expect this same phenomenon, this outrage cycle to play out every time regardless of truth or falsehood and regardless of the actual magnitude of the underlying issue. Because—because again what's being argued about is not the specifics about what actually happened, that's not the point. The point is the ability to form up moral tribes and then yeah and then have the rumble. Yeah, and just to that end too, when people say, "Oh Jussie Smollett," that is fresh in my mind. There's George Orwell actually has a—a great thing on this. Because George Orwell famously covered the Spanish Civil War which was kind of—as I said—is kind of the original where a lot of this stuff sort of played out for the first time in the 20th century. And he—he—he did this thing one time where he talks about his version of this—he talks about the role of the atrocity. And he said basically the—the key point of propaganda in any war—so you get two countries that go to war and you know they've each got a moral claim of like why they're the justified—you know nobody ever attacks everyone's always defending—like and I only attacked him first because he was going to attack me otherwise. And so every—each side in a war, each country, has its own basically moral position why the war is justified. But he said—he said basically Orwell said—like it's hard to sell like high—it's hard to sell like these high-blown—you know these high-flying kind of abstract ideas of like why we need to go to war. And so he said what happens is the propaganda efforts of each government in a war become basically looking for atrocities. And so specific point events. A famous one is the My Lai Massacre in Vietnam but you we could—we could name many many other examples. And so you're looking for these very specific events where something very specific and concrete happened that's clearly over the moral—over beyond the realm of moral acceptability. Just clearly like in—you know usually involving innocent people getting killed. Right. And in the Spanish Civil War it was like, you know, nuns getting killed or something like that, right? And—and the—again it's the point of the atrocity—the point of the atrocity is not that it affected a hundred thousand people. The point of the atrocity—in fact maybe the best atrocity only affects a small number of people because you want to personalize it. And then he said—he says this. He says the truth or falsity of the atrocity doesn't matter at all. Like it—the atrocity—if the atrocity is made up, the political value of the atrocity is just as high. Right? And so it doesn't matter—like once the atrocity cycle gets running it doesn't matter whether the underlying thing is true or false. Because the—because the point of it is to have the—have the atrocity be a propaganda win for moral—for moral justification and then for moral demonization of the other side. And by the way, lest—lest people think that I'm being cynical when I go through all this, I'm not saying I think this is all good. Like I'm not saying I think that this world is great. I'm not saying that this—I think that this is all positive. I'm saying something different, which is I think it's very important to understand how this actually works. Because when you find yourself in the middle of one of these things, I think it's very useful to have at least a slice of your brain that's basically saying, "All right, I need to be aware of the fact that I'm getting pulled into something." Right, that I'm being triggered by emotion and I'm being triggered and I'm being slotted into a moral tribe and I'm mounting up to go to war with the hated enemy and it's on the basis of something that very well might not be true. Right, and I think—and this—you know this is a René Girard thing which he sees—this scapegoating cycle always works because people always forget that there's a scapegoating cycle. Like—like whenever you have to—like crucify the scapegoat, it's always the most morally important thing that's ever happened for the duration of that cycle, right, until it's forgotten by the next one. And so, so a big part of what I'm trying to do is explain why, as we surf all of these current things that we like, we should at least keep part of our brain that basically says, "Look, this is the state of the world we live in, this is the state of humanity, this is the state of the media. Like, this is what's happening. We can like it or hate it or we can debate the pros and cons, but it is what's happening, and all objective events are going to be filtered through that." To the extent that you care about objective truth, you have to get through that and you have to get to the other side and be able to actually still deal with objective reality. But to do that, you need to understand the cycle. And it's also important to understand because if people are ever involved in the cycle or implicated in it, they typically say, "Oh, but I'm innocent. Once everyone finds out the truth, they're going to exonerate me, they're going to clear me." They, of course, don't fully appreciate that people don't give a shit. People are, you know, predisposed to hate you and they'll just find any sort of reason, you know, or certain people perhaps. Yes, yes, yes, if yes, if you are—yes, if you are actually an—if you are actually innocent and if you're caught up in one of these things, it's horrifying, it's terrifying. I mean, look at cancellation. I mean, this happened to a lot of people over the last decade. You know, people would get canceled for things they literally never did and for, you know, for people then imputing beliefs or, you know, some I don't know, stray comment or misreported comment. And then it gets imputed into "you know, this person is a horrible racist" and off and away it goes. And they have to get fired and purged from society and, you know, have no way to come back. Or, you know, or whatever the issue was, right? And, you know, we, you know, there were these moments, you know, these viral video—yeah, oh, that's the other thing is like, okay, the viral videos. The viral videos, right? The viral videos have become so much like the—by the way, the first viral video that mattered was actually Rodney King. And it was, and it actually—and I'm not going to, I don't want to litigate Rodney King, good or bad or whatever it was, the whole separate conversation—but the Rodney King video starts with the beating. So it doesn't show everything that led up to that. And that set the pattern. And again, I'm not, I'm not excusing anybody for anything or whatever, I'm just saying, like, the video started midway through the event. All of the really effective viral videos basically since then, including the ones that have been really important over the course of the last decade, they've all had that characteristic. They all start halfway through the event. So you don't see the run-up. And so you don't—you know, the Central Park bird watcher was the famous, you like—you don't see the run-up of what happened before the video started. What was the other one? Who was the other person who got like brutally canceled based on a video? I can't remember. I mean, there have been a whole bunch of them. But this is the thing, right, and so you, you get a viral video and it kicks off halfway into the event. Why does it, by the way, why does it kick off halfway into the event? That's because that's when the event became interesting enough to be able to put on a video. Like, that's when people don't just like walk around with their phones out; that's when they pull the phone out. But by that point, you've lost all the context, right? And so, and again, this is one of those things where, okay, you're watching a viral video and you can feel yourself getting outraged. The key question ought to be, "All right, what happened before that?" Right? And you can—and that takes work, right? Like, because it's not, it's not right there on the screen, and you have to like really try hard to like go back and like figure out eyewitness reports. And by the way, like, if it's a criminal thing, half the time you don't find out what happened until the actual trial happens, so you don't find out for like another year. Because they don't put the evidence out until the trial. And so that—that's another thing where people get basically—people get used basically. People get ginned up into these outrage cycles without actually having the context for what happened. The—I want to go back to this conspiracy. You know, we have a friend who thinks everything is an op, and maybe I'm naive and I miss out on the ops that are actually there. But I'm curious what mental model one might have to think about how much is coordinated vs not. I've been inspired by the idea of decentralized or emergent collusion, just sort of this idea that a lot of people have incentives to go in the same way. Even if they're not coordinating explicitly, they almost might as well be, or something like that, or it might explain why so many things seem coordinated. But, you know, ever since joining a16z, so many people have impugned onto us things that we're not doing, so it's made me skeptical of people calling for conspiracy. And so I'm curious what sort of mental model you might have for the idea of ops and how that might help one understand the social platforms today? Yeah, so I think as best I can tell—and it's tricky because it's hard to tell, and there are, you know, some real experts in this that really, really focus on this—but as far as I can tell, like, there's some, there's some truth on both sides. The answer is messy, and it's messy for two reasons. So messy number one is just like there are a lot of things that I think are happening that are truly organic and then there are a lot of things that are happening that are ops. And I think, I think there's a combination. And then I think—maybe you're alluding to this—and then there's this thing which is like something can start as an op and then become real, right? Which is kind of the point of the op, right? If you—let's put it this way: if you and I are going to be Machiavellian and we wanted to start a movement, right, and we wanted to start a moral outrage cycle because we had some underlying goal, because we were either trying to win an election or trying to get a political something, you know, a law passed or something like that. We were going to like—we're maximum like Machiavellian deviousness and we're going to like have a black budget, you know, and we're going to pay influencers to lie and all this stuff that people say, you know, bot—we're going to have our bot army and so forth. Like, the point of it is not to just like have a bunch of propaganda online with a bot army behind it. The point is to get people to actually believe it, right? That is the goal, right? And so, and then, you know, you don't want to have to pay for like every post, right? At some point you want them to be happening organically even just out of a self-economic interest, and so you actually want—you're actually trying to generate the thing. Actually, there's a great paper on this. There's Timur Kuran, who's the famous sort of guy who talks about preference falsification, and then Cass Sunstein wrote this thing years ago where they talk about this phenomenon relevant here; they call it availability cascades. So their term for this kind of thing that we've been talking about, which is like a viral outrage cycle, their term for it they call availability cascade. And what they mean by availability cascade is sort of—cascade means basically the process. It's the sociological term for sort of an idea or concept kind of cascading through a society, kind of building up momentum as it goes. And then availability they call is based on the idea of availability bias, which basically is we tend to over-focus on the thing that's right in front of us. And so we tend to over-focus on the thing that's basically present in our mind at that time, which may or may not be as important as other things that we're not focused on. But, but in the paper, the significance of it is, in the paper, they identify the role of a specific kind of person they call availability entrepreneur. And the role of the availability entrepreneur is to try to inject into the public consciousness availability bias as a deliberate exercise to basically say, "This is the specific thing that you should be focused on right now." Like, this is the specific person who's been hurt by something, this is the specific whatever, you know, moral event that's happened, this is the specific issue, this is the specific thing. And basically what they say is like, every time you see an availability cascade, if you trace it back, there was always what they call the availability entrepreneur, which was basically the person who was like, "Aha, I am going to put..." Oh, I'll give you the classic example. And again, this is not a value judgment and, you know, this was like, you know, fantastically good that this happened, but Rosa Parks was the classic example of this. Like, the story that we all got told in school was Rosa Parks was just like another random, you know, sort of a random person, you know, with the thing on the bus. Rosa Parks was like an actual—she was a trained activist. Like, they—she went to—like, there was this whole like school of activists and they—like, she was like a specifically trained... it was an availability entrepreneur who set up an availability cascade targeted on Rosa Parks that ended up causing fundamental and obviously very, very positive changes to American society. But like, it started as an op. Like, she started as, she started as an op. But the point of the op was not to be the op; the point of the op was to become what happened, which was to become a nationwide movement that ultimately led to, led to fundamental change. Okay, so anyway, the point is like just because things start as ops doesn't mean they're not real, right? And if—and if the op leads to real outcomes, then you might as well con—you know, it's like a m—at some point it's just a matter for historians of how it started. Like, it's a footnote to the Rosa Parks story today that she was a trained activist. It doesn't really matter because she—because that op unlocked a broad-based, actual movement in American society that led to actually profound change. And so this is the problem with this topic, which is "Oh, that's just an op" is basically like cope. Like, okay, right? It might have started as an op, but if it ends up in a real thing, it doesn't matter, you see what I'm saying? It doesn't matter that it started as an op. And then, by the way, conversely, it also doesn't matter if it didn't start as an op, because again, it—it's the same outcome. And so, so I do think I think both things are true. I do think there's this furious fight. I do think there are serious operators and, you know, you can go online and you can find out about these things. This is a big issue, by the way, in political influencer world right now. This is—this is actually a big thing. So if I want to run an advertising campaign to sell shampoo, legally in the US if I hire influencers to say good things about the shampoo, they—I believe this to be true—they have to disclose that it's a paid commercial. They can't just like pretend that they just happened to stumble on this shampoo. If I donate money to a political candidate, I have to disclose that as part of election—federal election law. If I pay an influencer to take a position on a moral topic that is not a product that is sold or a political candidate, but is just like a position on a moral or political thing, that doesn't qualify either as an advertisement or as a political donation. It's fully legal to do that in the dark. Well especially AI, right? A lot of—a lot of paid doom out there. We were just talking about Aila's, you know, a new media fellowship for doomers and yeah, it's a perfect example and it, yeah, seems to be having a real impact. This is a great example. So this is the thing where, you know, they're—they're living the meme, right? They're living the meme because—so this is a great—so, so I don't know if you, you may have covered all this already, but yeah, so like, they're—we, we know we're in this AI—we're in this pitched AI policy battle. And we, we know through that, and we, we're deep into this, we know that there's dark money in, in AI. Like a lot of the AI doomers and stuff is basically bought and paid for. Like, so there's these, these influence operations that are happening, and they fall into this bucket of—it's, it's neither an advertisement to say AI is going to kill everybody, nor is it a political candidate. And so you—there are organizations and companies that are paying the, you know, there's this... And again, not everybody who says that is—is being paid, but there, there is a lot of dark money in, in that, in that world right now. And so, and of course, you know, the partisans in that world insist full-on, of course, that that is absolutely not happening until yesterday when they just go ahead and like live the meme and they just like announce the program. And so they announced the program in black and white right there on a website, and it's like a influencer camp in which you're going to go dot dot dot and, by the way, it's free, right? Which means, you know, you're—you're being paid. And then they say, "Where's the money coming from?" and they say, "Oh, you know, we're a non-profit being funded by, in this case, MIRI, which is one, one, one organization with its own kind of interesting funding history." And then they say, "And other donors," right. It's like, and it's literally, you know, it's literally like, it's is it—I don't know, is it a conspiracy if it's in plain sight? But and I'm just—I'm picking on that one because that's the one that happened yesterday. But like, that for sure is happening all throughout politics; it's for sure happening on lots and lots of social issues. It's—it's a thing. And so yeah, and so like, but again it goes back to this thing of like, okay yes, there are ops, yes, you can't believe what you see. By the way, the other—there are bot farms. Like, there are obviously bot farms. And there are obviously bot farms that are being deployed. You know, there are—there are online influencers, some of them very controversial, where you know, they clearly—there's clearly a bot army behind them. By the way, you could be an influencer online and have a bot army behind you and have nothing to do with the bot army, right? Like, somebody could be paying for the bot army and you might not even know about it. And again, today, it's totally legal to do that. Because again, if you're not selling a product and you're not donating to a political candidate, like, it's actually in this grey—it's actually fine to do. And so I think it's 100% the case that, like, there's ops all over the place and quite frankly, like, the longer I, I—the deeper I get into it, the more I'm like, yeah, there's probably more ops than I've, than I've, than I've been willing to acknowledge. But I would just say on the other side, it's like, I don't know, does it really matter? Like, is that really the thing that—because the ops are only going to work—be another way to put it is: the ops are only going to work if they, if they resonate into something that people are going to pick up and run with anyway. Like, to become a mass movement, this is what Timur Kuran says about the role of the availability entrepreneur—like, it's not enough to just like have your cause and then just like expect that you can kind of trick people into running with your cause. There has to be something to it that resonates with them, you know, kind of in them already, that cause—that causes them to like—and if maybe they pick it up and run with it based on a paid op and maybe—and by the way, maybe in the counterfactual they would have picked it up and run with it anyway, you know, without the op, and you know, you'll never know. That's a great mental model for it. Thinking about a last topic for today, I want to close on this idea, sort of the bridge between legacy and new media, because we see—just give some examples—obviously the *New York Times*, they've made the adjustment to the new world very well from a business perspective. You know, we had CBS acquire *The Free Press*, and so there's some, you know, acquisitions there, strategic acquisitions for legacy media companies who are trying to innovate. You know, on MTS ourselves we're sort of experimenting with—with players from both worlds. You know, we have new school podcasters and Twitch streamers. You know, Theo—is Theo—are you 21 years old? Yeah. 21, you know, he's born to be a, a Twitch streamer. But we also have—I mean, 21, I mean, over the hill. Exactly. Grey hair, grey hair, washed. Yeah, washed. But we also have Mark Halperin, who you know is, you know, legacy and legendary, you know, commentator and host, who—who funny enough, you know, we've talked about how Trump is—is the bridge figure in that he was successful both in legacy and in this new m—media climate, and it's rare for people to do both. Halperin, of course, interviewed him, did a very famous interview with him when he was talking about the Bible verses and, you know, getting him to mention some specific ones and you know, Trump, of course, didn't want to pick favorites. Anyway, so I'm curious how you think about this—sort of the bridge, either analysis of—of—of Trump as the bridge or more just, how do you see legacy players and new media players sort of playing out, or even the styles of what will work in this new, new format? Yeah, I mean, so I guess, I don't know, you maybe start with data, right? So the data shows that basically—focus on American data because it's what I'm most familiar with—so just in the data you basically see a couple things. So one is, trust in centralized institutions of all kinds is collapsing in the US. So basically any kind of legacy or incumbent institution, you know, trust is collapsing fast. By the way, that collapse in trust didn't start with the internet; it's actually started around 1970. It actually—it's part of this—it's part of this "WTF happened in 1971" meme is when basically trust in centralized institutions in the US started to fall off a cliff. That's true for many kinds of centralized institutions and it's been true for, you know, basically 55 years. But media's included in that. And so trust in centralized media is collapsing and, you know, keeps hitting new record lows basically every time they run the survey; it collapses further. And so, so that's certainly true. And then you, and then if—the business version of it is just very clear, which is—you know, we were talking about CNN earlier—ratings for all the centralized media properties are in full-scale collapse. You know, cable news is collapsing, you know almost every—almost every newspaper has collapsed, almost every magazine has collapsed, you know basically all the—all the legacy formats have collapsed, you know with only very rare exceptions. And so, you know, just that—and then by the way, correspondingly, it's just sort of obvious, you know, the rise of podcasts obviously is—is obvious now. And by the way, it's amazing; like, the election of '24 was the one where podcasts kind of flipped the—you know, went from being like, "I don't know if this matters" to like all of a sudden everybody's like, "oh wow, this matters like a lot." And so the rise of podcasts, of course, is undeniable at this point; the rise of social media, the rise of—of livestreaming, you know, these things are clearly happening. And so like, the—the changing of the guard, reordering of the businesses and of the cultural and social phenomena are clearly happening and, I think, are undeniable. You know, if you extrapolate forward, like obviously all, you know, essentially all of the remaining old media properties that don't figure out a way to adapt to the new world are going to, you know, they're—you know, going to get basically run by private equity and that'll be that. You know, which is kind of the story of the newspaper industry these days. So I, you know, that—like, it's almost like not even interesting to say that anymore just because it's like, it's so obvious that that's happening. And then obviously social media, you know, continues to grow as a phenomenon, keeps being very important. And so yeah, and you know, look, we're still—we're still living in this—we're still living in a hybrid world today, right? And so we're still living in a world in which, like, you know, to some extent it still matters what's on TV, but like, not nearly as much as it did. But, you know, pretty soon it's not—it's not going to anymore. So you know, so that world is changing fast. Yeah, I, I just think, yeah, the main thing is going to be, you know, the—the people who adapt to that are going to—they're either going to adapt or they're not. You know, you mentioned Mark Halperin, you know, who's a good friend of both of ours, you know, is—I think is doing like an amazing job adapting to that. And in a way that's just like, I would say, shockingly different and better than, you know, most of his former, former colleagues. You know, there's great examples. Then you get the *New York Times* has adapted to that in an interesting way. You know, the Hasan Piker apology newspaper has fully committed itself to—to the future, let's say. So you know, they're doing their thing. Yeah, you know, most of legacy properties won't adapt; they, you know, they never do. There's going to be lots and lots of new things that emerge. And maybe I should say here again to not sound—not sound cynical about this, like, it is really striking, I would say the following. So like, what are the two like mega, mega, mega trends in video and by the way, also in politics over the course of the last like five years? It's basically two things. It's short-form video in the form of TikTok, Instagram, and—and—and X, you know, short-form reels or short-form video. And in there you get like the full battery of accusations of like, you know, destroying attention span and everything's trivial. But on the other side is the rise of the three-hour podcast, right? And so on the podcast side you have this incredible phenomenon with three hours. And by the way, some of these, you know, Joe and—and Lex and others are now pushing this to the point where, you know, Lex now has like, in some cases, seven or eight hour podcasts, right? I think was it Balaji did like a ten-hour one on Lex. Yeah, so Balaji went for ten hours on Lex. And so and it's actually interesting because if you talk to those guys, they get data on completion rates and it's actually a very large percentage of their—of their listeners or viewers who actually listen to the whole thing. So like, many, many, many Rogan podcast or Lex podcast people actually listen to the full three hours. And so it's not—the thing I really reject is this idea that like—and I don't want people to think that I believe this is—like, this idea that everything is going to become just like trivial short-form social media bullshit like that. No, not at all. You have this corresponding thing which is you—it's like a barbell. You also have this—you have this massive rise in substance. And on the substance side I would point to the long-form podcasts, I would point to Substack. By the way, you know, there's a big push on X to have long-form essays. I would push, you know, online, online, online courses on every topic are available, and you just, yeah, you have this ability. Even AI—one of my favorite features of AI is what they call deep research, which is a feature on both—I think it's on particularly on—on ChatGPT and on Claude. But like, you set it loose and it will write you like, literally a 30-page, 30-page answer, you know, that's basically like a textbook on any topic. And so, so I think there is this other side which is you—you have this—you also have this growth in—you have this growth in depth. Oh, the other thing is just—and we're an example of this—the rise of the practitioner media vs practitioner-driven media. So people who are actually doing a thing showing up and talking about it, right? And of course, you know, that leads to howls of outrage of "that's not journalism if you don't have somebody asking like whatever super snarky critical questions about it or whatever." But like, the idea for somebody who is actually doing a thing—I mean, you know, Andrej Karpathy in AI, right, is just like shows up and like explains like, "here is how this all works" is just like amazing. And you know, in—in old media, like basically like when I was younger, like Charlie Rose did that. I grew up actually listening to Charlie Rose, watching Charlie Rose. But Charlie Rose literally was on at fucking midnight. Like, he was on CBS at midnight and he would have these hour-long conversations with these super interesting people doing super interesting things. But he was on at midnight and you had to stay up at midnight to watch Charlie Rose, right? And it was one of the great things about getting a DVR is you could record Charlie Rose and watch it during—during normal, normal daylight hours. But you know, we lost that, or we didn't have that for a very long year—we have that now. And so, so I do think it's worth noting like, there is this in my view clearly incredible, positive, informative, educational, super helpful, super constructive thing on the other side of it, which is of course a lot of what we try to do. Yeah. And you know, 2024 was the podcast election; it'll be interesting to see what 2028 will—people have been very concerned, you know, or sort of predicting, you know, deep fakes for a while and how that might influence things. But we haven't really seen anything there super material, it seems, but it'll be interesting to see what media formats or just how the 2028 election is—is different than the 2024 one in this conversation. We've had deep fakes forever. Like, people have believed all kinds of crazy bullshit. I mean, you know, was the—the Gulf of Tonkin incident that kicked off the—really kicked off the Vietnam War—like, I think we now know that that was a fake. Like, that's actually quite well established. And then some people might say Russiagate turned out—we now know it was a quite elaborate fake. So the—the deep fake thing I find very funny because it's like, it's not like we've been swimming in truth all this time and all of a sudden now, now there's going to be deep fakes. But anyway, that—maybe that's a separate conversation. The—I think the—the substantive comment I would make is so, so I actually think we have—people talk about like, famously talk about the 1960 election, Kennedy vs. Nixon, where television—where famously where television took over from radio, where if you—they had their famous debate and if you listened to it on the radio, you thought Nixon won, and if you watched it on TV, you thought that—that Kennedy won. So so you know, the audio vs. audio plus video. And so people have been talking for, you know, that was considered like the first television election where television was like the determining factor in who won or lost, along with, you know, maybe some shenanigans in Chicago. Anyway, speaking of deep fakes. So anyway, people have been talking for a while about when will there be the first internet election. And, and you know, people like to point to 2016 being, you know, Trump using social media, or maybe 2024 being the podcast election. So we've had three examples of internet mattering for elections, for sure. But I think we haven't had the true internet election. And as a consequence of that—and by the way, we therefore haven't really had the true internet candidate yet. And actually it's interesting as people talk today—this is not a pro or negative, pro or con comment on—on Trump himself for the purpose of this discussion, but people talk about Trump as the internet candidate, but he's really—he's really a hybrid in that he cares enormously about what's on TV. And he's on, and he's on TV a lot, and he watches TV all the time famously in order to—because TV is basically how he gets a read on the pulse of—of sort of what's happening and of what people think about things. And so like, I think Trump's kind of underlying theory is the things that are on TV are the things that the people running the TV network know that their viewers want to see, and so therefore if you watch it on TV, you can get a sense of what people actually think. And I, you know, and as TV has been the dominant medium for the last 60 or 70 years, you know, that's a pretty good theory; obviously it's worked pretty well for him. And then he—kicked—and then he also, like, cares a lot about like, affecting what's on TV. And there's this famous Trump story that lots of people have told where he'll be in a meeting and there will be a TV on in the background, he'll be watching it and he'll have it with the sound off, but he'll be watching the chiron—the, you know, the thing at the bottom of the screen, the text that shows what—what they're saying. And if he doesn't like what's on the chiron, he'll tweet something to change the subject, right? And so he'll tweet and then he'll literally watch and the chiron will change and he'll be like, "you know." So, right, he's able to single-handedly with his power of his Twitter to turn one current thing into another current thing, right? Okay. So, so my—but my point is like, he's a hybrid. So he's very social media fluent, but he's also very television. And of course his entire career was made on television. So he's very television fluent. He's one of the best, you know, television programmers in history and he's a social media native. There will be an internet candidate. There will be an internet president who gets elected in, I believe, entirely based on the internet. Where they don't pay any attention at all to what's on TV, because it doesn't matter to them one bit and they're going to be entirely online and they're not going to care what's in the—on TV and they're not going to care what's in the newspaper, and they're going to be 100% a creature of the internet. And I think we haven't—I believe we haven't seen that yet. I don't know when we're going to see it. I'm pretty sure it's possible it's '28; probably my guess is '32. Yeah, and so I think we all have—yes, for better or for worse—we all have that to look forward to. Yeah. The—well, I think that's a great note to—to wrap on. This has been a great discussion, all things media. Marc, thank you for coming on the first day of Monitoring the Situation. I am proud to be part of the monitoring. Excellent.
