And how do you orchestrate that at scale? It has not been done before. So this is what we have been focusing on a lot. It's amazing stuff and as you know, to sort of paraphrase you, it's not going to slow down. It's only going to get more and more amazing at least in the short term, right, so who knows what the long term has to bring. But just fascinating stuff. Best of luck to both of you and again Deepak and Abhinav, thank you so much for taking the time to join the podcast. Thank you so much for having us.

HCLTech Q4 FY26 Results Press Conference | #HCLTechQ4

HCLTech(28.5K) ·53:56 ·1.9K views · 2026-04-22
C Vijayakumar (CEO & Managing Director, HCLTech)
HCLTech concluded FY26 with $14.7B in revenue and 3.9% constant currency growth, signaling a strategic pivot toward "Advanced AI" which now contributes $620M in annualized revenue despite macroeconomic headwinds and softening discretionary spend in telecom.
TL;DR HCLTech concluded FY26 with $14.7B in revenue and 3.9% constant currency growth, signaling a strategic pivot toward "Advanced AI" which now contributes $620M in annualized revenue despite macroeconomic headwinds and softening discretionary spend in telecom. Revenue...

TL;DR

HCLTech concluded FY26 with $14.7B in revenue and 3.9% constant currency growth, signaling a strategic pivot toward "Advanced AI" which now contributes $620M in annualized revenue despite macroeconomic headwinds and softening discretionary spend in telecom.

FY26 Financial Performance and FY27 Outlook

  • Revenue Growth: HCLTech delivered consolidated revenue of $14.7 billion for FY26, representing 3.9% growth in constant currency.
      • Quarterly Decline: Q4 revenue stood at $3.7 billion, a 3.3% sequential decline driven by software seasonality and delayed procurement decisions in March.
  • Profitability Margins: Operating margin for the year was 17.2% (17.9% excluding restructuring costs).
      • FY27 Guidance: The company projects revenue growth of 1% to 4% and an EBIT margin of 17.5% to 18.5%.
  • Dividend Payout: The board declared an interim dividend of ₹24 per share, bringing the total LTM payout to ₹60 per share (97.6% of net income).
  • Headwinds: Two specific client challenges in the Americas are expected to create a 50-basis-point drag on growth in FY27.

The "Advanced AI" Growth Engine

  • Revenue Contribution: Annualized "Advanced AI" revenue reached $620 million, growing 6.1% sequentially in Q4.
  • Strategic Shift: CEO C Vijayakumar (CVK) emphasized a "New Math" model focusing on multiplying results rather than just adding headcount, with AI becoming integral to nearly every new deal.
  • Significant Wins:
      • AI Factory: A global tech major selected HCLTech for a $100M+ AI factory program to build next-generation data centers.
      • Physical AI: A semiconductor major engaged HCLTech for AI engineering services to support ASIC development.
  • AI Talent Categories: The company is reclassifying its workforce into three tiers: AI Builders (model fine-tuning), AI Superusers (3-4x productivity enhancement), and Human-in-the-loop Decision Makers.

Segment and Regional Highlights

  • Engineering and R&D (ERS): This segment led the growth with a strong 9.8% increase for the full year.
  • HCL Software: Reported $1.4 billion in revenue with an ARR of $1.05 billion. The segment is aggressively shifting from a perpetual license model to a predictable subscription-led model.
  • Geographic Resilience: North America remains relatively resilient, while Europe faces risks from rising energy prices, supply chain disruptions, and inflation.
  • Client Pyramid Growth: HCLTech expanded its high-value client base, adding one client in the $100M+ category and eight in the $50M+ category.

Workforce and Operational Efficiency

  • Headcount Growth: Total people strength reached 227,181, a modest annual increase of 1.7%, though revenue grew faster, indicating improved revenue per employee.
  • Fresher Onboarding: HCLTech added 11,744 freshers in FY26, a 50% increase over the previous year, focusing on building a cadre of "elite engineers."
  • Attrition: IT Services voluntary attrition improved to 12.5% (down 50 basis points year-on-year).
  • Restructuring Status: Restructuring efforts are largely completed, with any remaining synergies factored into the FY27 margin guidance.

Notable Quotes

"The future won't be built by adding more. It'll be built by multiplying what matters. The model has changed. It's a new math. A new mindset. And we are leading the change." — C Vijayakumar (CVK), CEO & Managing Director
"Our ambition is to be the best AI solutions company, leveraging our engineering pedigree... nearly every deal [now incorporates] an AI or Gen AI component." — C Vijayakumar (CVK), CEO & Managing Director
"We intend to reinvest the benefits of currency depreciation into strengthening our AI capabilities and our sales engine." — Shiv Walia, CFO
"Our revenue per employee continues to accelerate... our hiring plans will continue to be a function of how we plan to grow our business." — Ramachandran Sundararajan (Ram), Chief People Officer
More projects meant more people. More complexity meant more time. But that model is showing its age and its limits. Because in today's world, you can't solve new problems with old equations. And AI is changing the entire formula. The answer isn't just doing more, it's driving strong outcomes and big impact. At HCLTech, we're transforming how services are delivered and developing new solutions that help clients unlock new revenue streams. Supercharged by AI. Powered by people and best-in-class IPs that multiply possibilities. The future won't be built by adding more. It'll be built by multiplying what matters. The model has changed. It's a new math, a new mindset. And we are leading the change. We are multiplying results, not effort. We are Supercharging Progress. HCLTech Supercharging Progress™. Yeah, please. Yeah. So we are getting started. Thank you. Can everyone settle down please? And your phones on silent. Thank you. Good evening everyone. You know, we have the HCLTech leadership with us right now: CVK, Shiv, and Ram. And I'll hand it over to CVK to give you a color on the Q4 numbers and the overall performance for the full year. CVK. Good evening everyone. Thank you for joining us for HCLTech's Q4 and FY26 press conference. Before we get into the financials, I want to take a moment to reflect on an important milestone for all of us at HCL. This year marks 50 years of HCL Group. Five decades of building, reinventing, leading, and giving back. And while this is a milestone we are very proud of, it's also a starting point. The next 50 years begins now. It is so very fitting that as we mark this milestone, HCLTech has been named to Fortune's World's Most Admired Companies 2026 list. Coming to the business performance, as all of you know, FY26 began against a backdrop of uncertainty. Tariff-related volatility persisted, discretionary spending softened in the traditional segments while emerging in new areas, and client behavior reflected a continuous focus on cost optimization along with accelerated adoption of AI-driven productivity. Importantly, the momentum around AI remains strong, with nearly every deal incorporating an AI or Gen AI component. Our ambition is to be the best AI solutions company, leveraging our engineering pedigree. Against this backdrop, FY26 marks the third consecutive year in which HCLTech will be delivering industry-leading organic growth among all the scale players. I extend my sincere appreciation to all HCLTechies for their continued dedication and outstanding performance. In FY26, we delivered consolidated revenue of 14.7 billion dollars, representing 3.9 percent growth in constant currency. And all the growth rates hereon referred will be in constant currency. The performance was supported by increasing contribution from advanced AI and broader AI offerings. Our advanced AI annualized revenue reached 620 million dollars. Our operating margin for the year was 17.2 percent and excluding restructuring cost, margins stood at 17.9 percent, which is a decline of 40 basis points from the previous year. In FY26, our services business grew 4.8 percent led by steady momentum in both our IT and Business Services which grew 3.7 percent, while our Engineering and R&D delivered a strong 9.8 percent increase. In HCL Software, revenue stood at 1.4 billion dollars, with an ARR of 1.05 billion dollars, reflecting a decline of 4.1 percent year-on-year and 0.5 percent year-on-year on the ARR. Coming to our Q4 performance, our revenue for the quarter was at 3.7 billion dollars, a decline of 3.3 percent quarter-on-quarter and a growth of 2.4 percent year-on-year. Our services business declined 0.1 percent sequentially but grew 4.2 percent year-on-year. HCL Software declined 28.1 percent sequentially and 14.1 percent on a year-on-year basis. Our operating margin for the quarter came in at 16.5 percent, or 17.7 percent excluding restructuring. Apart from the sequential decline due to the seasonality in the software business, we saw a delay in procurement decisions in the month of March that resulted in revenue coming below our expectations. At the same time, our advanced AI revenue reached 155 million dollars, reflecting a 6.1 percent sequential increase. Our services performance this quarter came in at the lower end of our expectations. This was primarily driven by a reduction in discretionary spending on Telecom, as well as the discontinuation of two SAP programs which happened late in the quarter. While annuity programs in Telecom held steady, we saw select clients scale back discretionary investments across both digital business and engineering services during the quarter. Some of this impact is likely to carry into the next quarter, and we've factored that into the guidance that we will be providing. After two quarters of strong new bookings, our new deal bookings for the quarter moderated to 1.93 billion dollars. Booking momentum for the fiscal was good; TCV of net new booking for the year clocked 9.3 billion dollars, which is the same as last year. If you look at our client categories, we've grown them across the board, starting with an increase of one for greater than 100 million dollar clients, eight for greater than 50 million dollar clients, and all the way at the 1 million dollar range where we added 28 clients. This is a great proof of our AI strategy and our client relevance in the AI era. In our advanced AI, we did some very good wins. A global technology major selected HCLTech for an AI factory program worth over 100 million dollars. Our solution will fast-track the requirements of building and operating next-generation AI data centers to support cutting-edge AI workloads using the latest GPU technologies. A global semiconductor major selected HCLTech's AI engineering services to support ASIC development across multiple advanced node chips, strengthening its position in physical AI. As we close the quarter and fiscal year, our AI strategy is translating into deeper client engagement and clear market leadership. Momentum across our advanced AI offerings and overall AI portfolio remains strong, reflecting the strength of our early bets and our continued focus on AI that scales from experimentation to measurable business impact. Our pipeline remains robust and broad-based across segments, verticals, and regions, with AI increasingly integral to nearly all deal conversations. The business environment remains highly fluid, making it difficult to form a definitive view of how the next 12 months will unfold. We're seeing second-order effects play out; rising energy prices and supply chain disruptions are weighing on the growth outlook in Europe, with risks of inflation and industrial slowdown becoming more pronounced. At the same time, North America continues to show relative resilience with no broad-based macroeconomic challenges at this stage. While our business fundamentals remain strong, we do have two client-specific challenges in Americas that will create a headwind of approximately 50 basis points to growth in FY27. These clients are navigating their own business pressures in the current macro environment and have scaled back their IT and business operations spending. In our software segment, we remain focused on accelerating the shift from a perpetual model to a more predictable subscription-led model. On the margin front, the underlying margins of our portfolio remain resilient, as reflected in our EBIT margins excluding restructuring. As we navigate specific growth challenges in FY27, we intend to reinvest the benefits of currency depreciation into strengthening our AI capabilities and our sales engine. Our guidance for financial year 27 is as follows: the revenue growth guidance for the full year is 1 to 4 percent in constant currency and 1.5 to 4.5 percent for services. EBIT margin guidance is at 17.5 to 18.5 percent. Now I will request Shiv to walk you through more details on our financial performance. Thank you. Thank you CVK. Good evening everyone. Thank you for joining our Q4 financial year 26 earnings call. Let me walk you through our financial performance for the quarter first. Total revenue for the quarter is rupees 33,981 crore, which is a growth of 0.3 percent quarter-on-quarter and 12.3 percent on a year-on-year basis. Coming to the services side, services revenue came in at rupees 31,230 crore, which is a growth of 3.5 percent quarter-on-quarter and 14.2 percent on a year-on-year basis. The software revenue for the quarter came in at rupees 2,857 crore, which is a drop of 24.6 percent quarter-on-quarter and 5 percent on a year-on-year basis. Moving on to profitability for the quarter, our EBIT came in at rupees 5,620 crore at 16.5 percent of revenue. On the back of a seasonally strong OND quarter for HCL Software, our EBIT dropped by 10.6 percent quarter-on-quarter and grew 3.3 percent on a year-on-year basis. Net income for the quarter is at rupees 4,488 crore at 13.2 percent of revenue. Now moving on to our financial performance for the financial year 2026, starting again with the revenue performance. Total revenue for the year is at rupees 1,30,144 crore, which is a growth of 11.2 percent on a year-on-year basis. Coming to the services side, services revenue came in at rupees 1,18,158 crore, which is a growth of 12.1 percent on a year-on-year basis. Software revenue for the year came in at rupees 12,397 crore, which is a growth of 2.9 percent on a year-on-year basis. Moving on to the profitability, our EBIT came in at rupees 22,397 crore at 17.2 percent of revenue, down 107 basis points year-on-year. Our EBIT grew 4.6 percent on a year-on-year basis. Net income for the year is at rupees 17,361 crore at 13.3 percent of revenue. EBIT and net income exclude the one-time impact of the new labor code. Including the same, FY26 EBIT is at rupees 21,441 crore and net income is rupees 16,642 crore. Moving on to return on invested capital ROIC, our ROIC continues to improve thanks to our ongoing focus on profitability and efficient capital management. The last 12 months ROIC is at 40.3 percent for the company, up 235 basis points year-on-year, and services ROIC now is at 47 percent, up 155 basis points year-on-year. Software continued to improve with ROIC at 22.6 percent, up 274 basis points year-on-year. Now let me share the details on our strong cash generation. Our cash generation remains very strong. Over the last 12 months, operating cash flows reached rupees 19,975 crore while free cash flow generation was at rupees 18,553 crore. Our balance sheet remains strong with gross cash at rupees 33,447 crore and net cash at rupees 33,288 crore. Operating cash flow to net income conversion is healthy at 115 percent and free cash flow to net income conversion is at 107 percent. Update on DSO: our total DSO including unbilled is currently at 84 days, which is an increase of three days over the previous quarter. And now for the shareholders. The diluted EPS for the last 12 months came in at rupees 64.01, which is down 0.1 percent year-on-year. Including the impact of the new labor code, the diluted EPS is rupees 61.36. The board has declared an interim dividend of rupees 24 per share for the quarter. The record date is 25th of April '26 and the payment date of the same shall be 5th of May '26. That brings our last 12 months payout to rupees 60 per share, effectively distributing 97.6 percent of our net income for this year. That's all from my side for now. And I would like to hand over the discussion to Ram for an update on HR matters. Thank you. Thank you Shiv. Good evening everybody. Am I audible? Okay. Good evening everybody. For the quarter ending March '26, our people strength is 2,27,181. That's a sequential increase of 802. On a full year basis, our people strength increased by 3,761, that's a 1.7 percent increase annually. If you look at it, the revenue grew at a faster pace, implying the revenue per employee continues to accelerate. This quarter we added 1,712 freshers. The number is very similar to what we added in the JFM quarter last fiscal. On a full-year basis, we added 11,744 freshers, that's about 50 percent more than what we did in FY25. If you recall at the beginning of FY26, we did say that our plan for FY26 is to significantly increase the fresher hiring, and that pretty much is what we have done through the year. IT services voluntary attrition trailing 12 months is at 12.5 percent. On a full year basis from FY25 to FY26, that's a 50 basis points improvement. With that brief, I'll hand it over to Ashu. So we'll open the floor for questions. Identify your publication and your name, yeah. And if you can ask multiple questions in one go. Sure, sure. Hi, this is Urvashi from Fortune India. First of all, congratulations on five decades. I want to understand why the guidance band is so wide and what are the indicators for such a wide band? Yeah, I think even last year we gave a 3 percent band for guidance. And normally it was 2 percent many years ago. But given the volatility in the market, we are a little more comfortable with a broader band. And we have certain assumptions for our lower end, mid-end and high-end. Our lower end assumes similar discretionary spending which has been soft especially since March. And we talked about client-specific reductions. So we have factored that in and I mean we are assuming that might get even worse during the year. That's the low-end. Midpoint we think similar discretionary spending environment and the two clients will have whatever planned reductions that we have, that's what will happen. In the third, the higher end, discretionary spending environment will improve and also we will have a very strong booking in Q1/Q2 because of a couple of large deals. So I think we've baked in all the scenarios and given you a 3 percent guidance band. Okay. And can you shed some color on the margins of the advanced AI revenue? And last question, what are some reasons behind the decline in the revenue of the top five clients? Yeah, I think advanced AI revenue, it's a little premature to call out the margin. So right now we have a very strong mechanism for tracking. And if you look at the top five clients, I mean we did call out it's largely because of one client, for whom we had a very huge uptick in FY25. And now because of offshoring, its FY26 revenues are lower. Otherwise, if you see, all the client pyramids have grown very strongly as I mentioned. So we continue to increase the revenue from these clients despite some of the deflation from AI. Thank you. Hi, good evening. This is Tanya from the Economic Times. Few quick questions for you. Your AI revenue grew even as overall services growth stayed, you know, weak. And AI is one of your main growth drivers. So I just wanted to ask, are you seeing it reduce demand for some traditional services work as well? Yes, I think more than the demand is reduced, I think whatever was there, there is a deflation, right? There is demand, of course; our pipeline is strong, but all of them is getting influenced by AI. So we don't call the traditional demand influenced by AI as advanced AI revenue. So I don't see that the demand for traditional services has reduced, but let's say something was a 100 million dollar deal, now could be an 80 million dollar deal because of the deflation. And AI continues to be—we've taken some early bets. We have a very rigorous way to classify our advanced AI revenue. And we are very happy to report 620 million of annualized revenue and growing at 7 percent. Recently you made some acquisitions. It was Finergic in Jan and there were some other in late last year. What kind of contribution have they made to FY27 guidance? Yeah, Finergic was a very, very small acquisition and it will hardly have a 3-4 million dollar impact in FY26. In this 27 it will have maybe 3-4 million dollar. It's not something to really—in the larger scheme of 14.7 billion, it's not relevant. But the two new acquisitions that we announced in December or in the OND quarter, one was JasperSoft and the other one was a carveout of the telecom business unit from HPE. Both of them are still pending clearance. So there is a little uncertainty as to when the clearance will come because some of this is dependent on the US government approvals. So right now we have not factored in anything. Whenever we get the approvals then we will let you know. One last question. You recently partnered with clients like Western Union on some new AI focused capability centers. Some recent data shows that GBSCs are facing a nearly 40 percent AI talent gap. So are you seeing clients struggle more with finding talent than with setting up the actual centers, and could this probably be a bottleneck in the next couple of years? Yeah, I think when you talk about AI, talent is one of the most critical requirements and there is definitely a huge talent gap in getting the right type of skills. And more than anything else, I think we need to transform the talent that we have. So we have a very clear talent strategy where all our people will get classified into three categories. One is AI builders, who can build solutions, who can work with models, fine-tune, and create completely new AI solutions. And the second category is what we call as AI superusers, who can enhance productivity to 3 to 4x from their current productivity levels. The third category is what we call as human-in-the-loop AI decision makers. They have very context-aware capability and can make decisions when there are multiple options provided by an AI solution. So that's how we want our talent pyramid to evolve. And we're working very diligently to kind of train and get our people in these three categories and really enhance the capability level. I'll just squeeze in one last question. There are recent discussions going on in the industry regarding the PoSH regulations and guidelines. As a company, have you also sort of tried to revisit the mechanism and what are your thoughts on that if you could shed some light on that? Yes, I think firstly, what has come out and what has been talked about is very unfortunate. I don't think anybody sitting in any management team will ever stand on the side of turning their eyesight away from what has happened. So just to be very clear, we do take it very seriously. To your specific question of what have we done, we do have a very robust process which I'm sure is the case with every other large enterprise. What we have done additionally is to be able to do what I call as ACE audits. ACE stands for adequacy, compliance, effectiveness, right? It's one thing to have the right policy, set out the right procedures, build the right channels, create awareness, and make sure that those processes work. But it's also important to get it independently audited and verified to see whether what we have is adequate, and if we are in compliance with what we set out to be. It's not just about compliance to what the statute says, but more importantly what we set out as what we want to do, whether we are adhering to that and is it effective? So one thing that we have done and we continue to do is to do this as an independent audit. And that gets reported at the highest level of the management to have management attention to see whatever we need to do. So yeah, that's where we stand. Anyone else over here? Hi, this is Rishabh Bhatnagar with NDTV Profit. Firstly, I want to ask, are you seeing clients shifting from output-based pricing to a billing-hour pricing sort of a structure? If that is so, what is the impact in revenue growth and pricing structures that you're seeing? Yeah, that's the first one. Yeah, I think obviously because of AI there is more shift towards output and outcome-based pricing than input-based pricing. Right now I have nothing to report on the revenue impact positively or negatively. But that is a trend and we are also very strongly advocating and providing solutions which are more outcome-based solutions for our clients. Staying on AI, have you seen any sort of cannibalization for legacy revenues yet? And how do you see the volume to sort of offset that issue? Yeah, I think yes, we've talked about it. We will see a deflation in some of the existing revenue streams. And based on our portfolio, AI deflation could be 2 to 3 percent a year. But we have launched a lot of new AI services, which is what we are calling as advanced AI. This has got five broad categories of services. One is AI factory, physical AI, semiconductor design for inferencing, then AI-led marketing services, and IP and platform. So these five are what largely contribute to our advanced AI revenue. And as you can see, it's grown 6 or 7 percent sequentially. We think this is a strong revenue stream; it should grow 25-30 percent year-on-year. This will really become a big growth driver. And we have disproportionate emphasis among the leadership to really drive the advanced AI revenue. We are also doing all the right things like AI transformation in our existing customers to transform our traditional revenues into a more AI-led operating model. Right. And lastly one on Middle East exposures. How is that situation evolving for you, what sort of feedback are you now getting from clients? Our Middle East exposure is very small, in line with our overall rest of the world revenue which itself is about 9 percent, so Middle East will be maybe sub 1 percent revenue contribution to the overall numbers. We saw some impact in the software business during the quarter; some of the procurement decisions got delayed. We haven't seen anything in the services business. Good evening, I'm Sakshi Jain from Informist Media. One quick question. What are the company's investment priorities in FY27, especially when it comes to acquisitions, partnerships, and building AI infrastructure, including training? Yeah, I think like last year we did spend almost 50 basis points of our revenue into AI-led investments and go-to-market investments. Similar things will continue this year. And if you look at the overall R&D spend of HCL, it's somewhere between 2 and a half to 3 percent of our revenue. A lot of that is going towards modernizing our products using AI and building new services which are advanced AI services. That's what it is. One maybe on hiring. Target for FY27, especially for the break-up for freshers. So you're specifically referring to overall hiring or is it in the context of... It would be great if you could help us with the overall number and the break-up for freshers. So overall hiring is always going to be a variable number. It's very difficult to give an annual forecast for that. Generally it works on the basis of quarterly and monthly run rates. Entry-level hiring is going to be on similar lines as what we have done for this year. Like I said last year, in the current environment, trying to put out a number on a full year basis is not going to be the wise thing to do. It's always going to be managed on a quarterly basis, a rolling quarterly basis. But overall, directionally, if we look at it, it'll be along the similar lines as what we did in FY26. All right. Thank you. Okay, so now we'll move to media who have joined us virtually. So over to you Nitin. Thank you Ashutosh. For our friends who have joined us virtually today, please follow these instructions to ask your question. Our first question is from Rishabh Shah from Moneycontrol. Rishabh, please go ahead and ask your question. Gentlemen, Rishabh here from Moneycontrol. So I have two questions. HCL announced restructuring in the previous quarter. Could you give a color as to how many employees would be impacted overall? And second, about 130 HCLTech employees were affected in one of your US locations. So was it because of a client ramp down or was it insourcing by the GCC? Yeah, so we have discussed this earlier. We are not calling out any numbers as a part of this. And whatever we have in the US, maybe Ram you can... Yeah, I think it was very specific to one engagement; that was part of the plan for service delivery transformation. That's how the deal was constructed, that's part of the plan. I don't think there is anything unusual that has happened. If I connect the dots, I think your question is about the incident or the action that happened in Florida. That's specific to one particular engagement, that was part of the deal structure, and that's on the basis of how the service delivery was designed to be. Got it. Thank you, thank you sir. Thank you Ram. Our next question is from Jas from Mint. Jas, please go ahead and ask your question. Hi, am I audible? Good evening sirs. Just want to understand with the 1 to 4 percent revenue guidance, you've mentioned that AI deflation would be 2 to 3 percent per year and also on the point that a deal that was once sold for 100 million could now come for 80. In this light, how are you looking at growth going ahead? Also if we consider the fact that GCCs are sprouting up and more of your clients are shifting the work in-house, how do you look at the growth environment overall going ahead? And second, on the fixed price contracts. How many of your contracts are more towards the fixed price and T&M contracts and how has this evolved or changed over the last three years? Thank you. Yeah, okay. So first is 1 to 4 percent growth and you overlay it with 2 to 3 percent deflation. That should really mean that the gross growth is probably much higher and we bake in the deflation and that's why it comes to 1 to 4. But Jas, I think our revenue is also categorized at a high level. Even the industry AI revenue, the total IT spend is getting categorized into three broad buckets. One is a set of traditional services which are all getting disrupted due to AI. Then there is a set of services which are getting amplified by AI like data, cloud, cybersecurity, and modernization. These are getting amplified. And then there are completely AI native services which is what we are calling as advanced AI. While the traditional services would decline, our TCV is also the same as last year. So it's not that—in spite of the deflation like the 100 coming to 80—if we have maintained the same total TCV that we won, broadly some 9.4 billion roughly, that's what we've maintained. Which means we've been able to offset the deflation and we've been able to win more to maintain the run rate. So that is going to create some growth. And in the AI amplified services like data, modernization, cloud, cybersecurity, we think they will have healthy growth and that could grow in close to double digits despite AI deflation. And the advanced AI revenue, we think it has potential to grow at least 30 percent for some time. So I think these different categories will all add up to eventually kind of drive the overall revenue categorization. I already addressed the question on TCV. Now the last question you asked was on FPP projects. If you can repeat your question—what exactly you wanted to ask on FPP? Right, sir. I just wanted to understand what percentage of your contracts are time and material, outcome-based and fixed price. And how has this evolved or changed over the last three years? I think it is slightly increased in the last year. I don't have the exact number, but last time when we talked about it, it was somewhat equally split. 50 percent is a little less than 50 percent on time and material or input-based, and a little more than 50 percent is more fixed price and managed services. Got it. Thank you, thank you sir. Thank you CVK. Our next question is from Debangana from Moneycontrol. Debangana, please go ahead. Hi, am I audible? Yes. Good evening gentlemen. CVK, just wanted to understand, you had alluded to the fact that the business environment right now is very fluid so it would be a bit unpredictable over the next 12 months. So just wanted to understand—is this, I mean are you seeing any deal cancellations because of that? Is there a delay in terms of deal renewals as well to that extent? And if you could shed some light on you slashing your full-year revenue growth guidance to 1 to 4 percent from what it was in FY26? I have a question for Shiv. Moneycontrol had reported some time a few weeks ago that HCLTech is looking to invest into Sarvam in a 250 million round along with Nvidia and Bessemer. Wanted to understand what synergies is HCLTech seeing through this deal and is there a timeline that you're looking at to close the deal? I think we're trying to talk about market speculations. So we continuously evaluate opportunities that create long-term value. If and when there is something material to share, we will definitely share. So right now I don't want to comment on the market speculation. From a guidance perspective, I mean I did talk about the environment being fluid, which is real and that's something everybody is seeing every day. There is a lot of volatility in a number of variables, geopolitics and things like that. And our guidance definitely is lower than what we started last year with. Last year was 2 to 5, this year is 1 to 4 and 1.5 to 4.5 in services. As I called out, apart from everything else, we have two client-specific situations. Due to the client's own business reasons they have planned significant ramp downs. That itself, at our plan levels, is a half a percent decline. And that's possibly an additional element to consider when you look at the overall guidance. I hope I have answered all your questions. I just had one more on delays in deal renewals and are you seeing any deal cancellations at the moment? On the software side, we did see significant delays in the procurement decisions in March especially. And we also, as I said, saw discretionary spend being reduced by two US Telecom firms, which impacted us in the last quarter and that is likely to play out during the rest of the financial calendar year. We also, as I said, saw two SAP programs were discontinued towards early March and towards the end of March. So there are some indications, but I'm not trying to kind of get a very broad-brush macroeconomic impact kind of thing. I'm trying to be as specific as I can so that you can make your own judgments. And just one last thing. You had also mentioned that you're seeing some changes in the operating margin; there was some impact because of the restructuring going on. Is this to be expected in the upcoming quarters as well or has all the restructuring been completed by now? It's largely completed. There could be some more in the next year but that's all factored into our operating margin guidance of 17.5 to 18.5 percent. Sure. Thank you. Thank you CVK. Our next question is from Veena Mani from the Times of India. Veena, can you hear us? Please go ahead and ask your question. Hi, Ram, I wanted to understand, you know HCL has been constantly saying that you're moving towards specialized freshers. Now does this mean that somewhere you're trying to look at only the top engineering colleges that can give you niche talent and tier 2, tier 3 college hiring will come down? And if you can help me understand also as more and more GCCs come up and clients trying to do their tech work in house, is it likely to have an impact on your onsite hiring? So I think our overall approach to hiring entry-level talent—we did articulate at the beginning of last year that we want to build a cadre of elite engineers. That approach is working well. If I look back at FY26, it has worked well and we will continue to do that. Now to your specific question of—does it mean that we'll engage with certain colleges and not engage with certain colleges? That is not the case. We do engage with a broad section of colleges and universities, largely to do two things, right? One, we are present pretty much across the country, both in tier 1 cities as well as in tier 2 cities, and we do want to cover all the locations that we operate in, so we do need to be a lot more broad-based. That's one. The other thing is also about fairness, right? It's about giving opportunity to everybody. We have set our entry criteria. Just because somebody comes from college A versus college B, we don't think that it's the right approach to deny opportunity to somebody coming from a different college. Yeah, the probability of success could be different depending on each individual, so our approach is broad-based. We do partner with and work with a variety of colleges and so far as our intake policy is concerned, it's based on who meets our entry criteria. So that's broadly what our approach has been. I think there's a second part to your question about GCC. Yeah, there is competition for talent. We've always dealt with competition; GCC is one more dimension to it. I don't think it's going to have an impact on our hiring plans. Our hiring plans will continue to be a function of how we plan to grow our business and what talent we need for our growth, so that's what is going to guide our approach to recruiting. One last thing about the restructuring. The last I think two quarters, this might be the third quarter that you're talking about a restructuring. How has it impacted your workforce and where all have you sort of tried to bring down people? So I think we need to look at—you're right, you know it's three quarters, I think at the end of Q1 FY26 is when we first talked about a restructuring. Just to recap, when we talked about restructuring we did very explicitly say that it's not just about people, it's also about other things, right? That's the most important thing to bear in mind. Now so far as people is concerned, if you look at the people strength that we have reported quarter on quarter, even on a full year basis our head count has only grown by 1.7 percent. So it's not a headcount decline that's happening. Which means you can backward the numbers; it's not about a massive headcount reduction exercise, it's a very targeted thing that we need to do. We also did call out that some of the things that we had to look at were inorganic transactions that we have done over the years. As we integrate and as we stabilize, it does give synergy opportunities. And that's what we focused on. So which is sort of globally distributed, so you wouldn't find any one country with some massive numbers being impacted because of restructuring. It's more efficiency-led measures. Okay thank you. Okay that's all for the press conference. Thank you for joining us. Thank you. Thank you everyone. Good evening.
